## Mêson de Savouè
### The House of the Whitehand

*The House of Savoy can trace it's lineage back to Humbert Whitehand, a local noble from Vienne, who was raised to the position as Count by the last King of Burgundy Rudolf III and pledged his service to Emperor Henry II, supporting his claim to the already disputed Kingdom. *

*In reward for his support, he was allowed to usurp Aosta from it's Bishops following a vacancy, with tacit approval by the Emperors, one of many small pieces in their growing feud with the Papacy and the first piece in establishing the core domain of the House of Savoy.*

*When Rudolf III finally passed, he once again supported Emperor Conrad the Elder in his claim to Arelat and for this service, the Maurienne, Chablais and Tarentaise, all taking from various surrounding Bishoprics were granted to him to form the County of Savoy, installing an Imperial loyalist in control of important Alpine passes.*

*Humberts younger son Otto once again enlarged the domain of the House by a substantial amount, inheriting the March of Turin through his wife Adelaide, notably in preference of living male relatives. Though their control of the whole march was brief, as after Adelaides death it largely broke up into various smaller territories, Piedmont remained with them and was joined to Savoy when he succeeded his elder brother.*

*More gains would only be made more than a century later in 1218, when the House of Zähringen died out, leading to the County of Vaud falling to the House by now known as the House of Savoy. Only two years later, the then Count Thomas the Elder would occupy the small town of Chambery alongside some other small territories, with it becoming the Capital of Savoy.*

## Baldwins early years.

Baldwin of Luxembourg, younger brother of the **first **Luxembourg Emperor of the **Roman Empire **. Is a Silver tongued and astute man. A noble man, but the second son, Baldwin was always destined for the church. And so Baldwin was sent off to be educated in theology and canon law at the preeminent University of Paris. The University of Paris being the [second oldest University in Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_medieval_universities), Baldwin would be highly influenced by the Romance education provided to him at Paris **and more importantly by proximity it furthered the ties between the House of Luxembourg and the French Royal House.**

Baldwin would not spend long at the University of Paris. In 1305 his family first tired to get him the vacant seat of the Archbishopric of Mainz, only to be rebuked in the attempt by a different Papal Appointment. But only two years later at the age of 22, Baldwins and the Luxembourg connection to the French Royalty were able to "pressure" the Pope into "appointing" the young Baldwin to the vacant seat at Trier.  Baldwins appointment at 22 was thusly based bluntly on nepotism if not by **simony**.

Trier nestled on the **Moselle river valley** between the Eiffel mountains and the Hunsrück range is the seat of an Imperial Archbishopric, **Imperial Chancellor of Gaul** and second to vote in Imperial Election (following Mainz whom votes first). Is an appointment that carries much weight in its prestige and ceremonial value rather than its economic value and territorial power.

-# Primary reference: https://www.lpb.rlp.de/fileadmin/files/Blaetter_zum_Land/Balduin_von_Luxemburg.pdf translated by Google Translate. Some of what is written is not entirely faithful to the source due in part to personal RP direction.

-# The diocese of Trier
-# ref: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electorate_of_Mainz#/media/File:Kirchenprovinzen_Deutschland_1500.jpg

## *Jann de Blannen*
-# John the Blind.

The story of Haus Luxembourg and Bohemia begins truly with **Heinrich von Gorizia**, who was brother-in-law to the last male-line Přemsylid King of Bohemia, **Václav III**. The assassination of Václav in 1306 meant the Bohemian estates gathered and elected Heinrich as king, owing to his marriage to Václav’ older sister, exacerbating the relationship Haus Gorizia had with Haus Habsburg, as the two split the post-Marchfield fractured Austrian duchies. Heinrich’s election was the will of the Bohemians, but not of his former Habsburg ally, Albrecht, the King of Germany, and thus the two quarreled as Albrecht intended to place his firstborn son, Rudolf, as the Bohemian monarch.
-# Heinrich von Gorizia’s coat of arms as King of Bohemia.

The Habsburg forces besieged Praha and expelled Heinrich, yet failed to sufficiently woo the estates. Rudolf’s ambition to take hold of the silver mines at Kutná Hora fed into a rebellion against him by the nobility, but he would ultimately die at the campsite of his attempted siege of Horažďovice in early Kuly, 1307. Heinrich was then re-elected king by the estates, should he submit to the charter they presented to him, and Albrecht’s renewed campaign to depose him was defeated—then, Albrecht’s own untimely demise would come at the hands of his kin in an assassination in 1308. 
-# Albrecht’s coat of arms as King of Germany.

Heinrich’s reign was seen as weak and wasteful by the estates who had elected him, and another party of opposition grew and sought cordial collusion with the newly elected King of Germany, **Heinrich VIII of Haus Luxembourg**, succeeding the late Albrecht. He arranged the marriage of his young fourteen year old son, Jann to a younger sister of Václav, **Eliška Přemslovna** in 1310. Following their marriage in Speyer, the couple was accompanied by agents of Heinrich VIII, being Berthold VII of Henneberg and the Archbishop of Mainz, Peter von Aichspelt, and in due course a contingent of soldiers from  Jann’s father, who was busied in preparations for his *Italienzug*. After being defeated by Heinrich von Gorizia on various occasions, Jann and his troops managed to successfully take Praha, forcing Heinrich and his wife to flee to their estates in Carinthia. 
-# Jann’s coat of arms as King of Bohemia.

Jann’s ascension as King of Bohemia was begrudgingly accepted by Leopold von Habsburg, Duke of Austria and Styria, after being threatened with Austria’s resubmission to Bohemia. The Habsburgs were confirmed by Heinrich VIII in their fiefs in return for Leopold accompanying him with a body of troops in the Italienzug, where upon his departure he declared Jann his deputy as imperial vicar for his absence. Unfortunately, Heinrich would meet his demise outside of Siena in 1313, and the ascendancy of Haus Luxembourg was stunted with his passing. Heinrich’s brother, Balduin, became the new head of the family as the Prince-Bishop of Trier, while Jann was merely seventeen and left to grapple with the Bohemian estates on his own, as he increasingly discarded his wife’s advice. Jann was considered too young by some, and too powerful by others, to be a potential candidate in the election for his father’s successor, and instead the Luxembourg party would take the side of the Wittelsbach Duke Ludwig IV after the contested election of Frederick von Habsburg, and ultimately Jann himself would participate in the Battle of Mühldorf which led to Ludwig’s capture of Frederick. 

Jann expanded the territory of the Bohemian crown through his alliance with Ludwig IV, from Cheb to Bautzen to Görlitz, but these successes poorly dressed his growing unpopularity at home in Bohemia. While Jann threw himself earnestly into German affairs, he was conversely seen as alien by the Czech nobility, and had himself been raised and educated in Paris around the French court. In the process of stabilizing the realm, Jann and the estates came to terse terms over the inaugural diplomas, which recognized the right of the nobility to elect the king, to decide upon extraordinary taxation, to be firm in the right to their property, the right to choose whether or not they shall give support to the king in his foreign wars, and successfully forbade the appointment of foreigners to the offices of the crown. In return, the aristocracy were merely encouraged to raise armies if the internal peace and defense of the kingdom was threatened. 
-# The Crown of Bohemia and the territories held in subject to, or in union by, the Luxembourg King.

Jann increasingly became an absentee ruler, preferring his estates in Luxembourg and foreign adventurers to the squabbling disputes in Bohemia where he felt his power too restricted, and came to rely upon competing noble parties for his government. Two dominant factions emerged, one beneath Jindřich z Lipé, and another beneath Vilém Zajíc z Valdek, and their story best illustrates the “stability” which Jann fostered under him. Jindřich had maneuvered first to become the king’s favorite, gaining the posts of chamberlain and Supreme Marshal of the Kingdom of Bohemia, before Vilém and Queen Eliška in 1315 convinced Jann of Jindřich’s alleged plot to elect another king, which resulted in Vilém’s authorization to arrest Jindřich, for which he took the latter’s positions. Then, in 1319, opposing nobles of Jindřich’s party convinced Jann of the same supposed rebellion-in-plotting but for Vilém, seeing the latter arrested and the former restored to his posts. This conspiracy was however expanded, accusing Queen Eliška specifically of having plotted with Vilém to overthrow Jann and install his son, the three year old Václav. For this, Jann demanded the seizure of Eliška from her castle of refuge, Hrad Loket, which he did by feigning a friendly visit to his wife to the guards at the gate. 

Jann had Eliška imprisoned at Mělník, while Václav (later, Karel) was kept in the dungeon at Loket for two months, when he was still merely three years old. Jann eventually reconciled with the queen to the extent of releasing her from her imprisonment, yet still took their eldest children out of her custody. Eliška went to live in Bavaria in exile, giving birth in 1323 to her final children fathered by Jann: the twin daughters Hana and Eliška. Václav was sent to Paris that same year, much as Jann had been in his own youth. When the little Eliška died in 1325, the Queen returned to Bohemia with Hana. Eliška would inevitably die in 1330, ill, with Václav having not seen her since 1319. 

Frustrated consistently with the insolence of the Bohemian estates and their plotting, which had resulted in various localized campaigns almost every time he was in Bohemia, such as being barred from Praha by rebels in the name of Eliška, Jann increasingly prioritized his foreign adventures abroad. He pursued his claim to the Polish throne, which he believed he inherited from his marriage to Eliška, by allying himself with the Teutonic Order against the Kingdom of Poland, having even fought in the 1326-1332 war against Władysław the Elbow-High, in which he vassalised many of the Silesian Duchies. Jann further even participated in multiple Teutonic actions against the Lithuanians, wherein he would eventually develop his blindness on-campaign in 1336. In his appreciation for the developing system of tourneys in Northern France, Jann would also dedicate a few weeks every year to his presence at the French court in Paris through the 1320s, staying within the Hôtel de Nesle which had been gifted to him. Václav, his eldest son, reached his age of confirmation there, and took the name **Karel**, in honor of his grand uncle **Charles IV** of France. 
-# Karel vu Lëtzebuerg.

Jann only really returned to Bohemia to raise funds for his escapades and sojourns in Germany, France, and Poland, and in 1330 it was in preparation for his most ambitious campaign yet: Italy. The rise of Mastino II della Scala of Verona threatened the peace and order of the North Italian cities, and in reminiscence of his father, Jann crossed the Alps in yet another entanglement with adventurism, yet this time in claim of restoring peace and dignity to the area. The city of Brescia, which had once fiercely resisted the campaign of his father, Heinrich VIII, willingly opened its gates to him as a means of protection against Verona. Jann gained patronage over a collection of cities in Lombardy afterwards, alleging the acquisition of a signoria therein while his former ally, Ludwig IV, grew distrustful of him and told the Italian princes and cities to obey only his own imperial vicar, Otto von Habsburg. Jann used his position within the empire, and certainly within Italy at the time, to negotiate arrangements with the new Valois King of France, Philippe VI, after he had already arranged the marriage of Karel to Blanche de Valois in 1329. Their arrangements in 1332 were a committance of Jan to Philippe’s side in conflict which did not include the King of Germany, and their courts further bound.
<@460814348487753730>

The fragility of Jann’s campaign in Italy, just a few years after Ludwig IV’s own ventures, highlighted the greatest defender of the peninsula yet against the Germans: **Robert d’Anjou**, King of Naples. Robert had resisted Jan’s father Heinrich VIII, he had resisted Ludwig IV, and his northern partnerships would eventually harass Jann’s position therein and compel him to withdraw in 1333. He left his son Karel over the positions they still held, but he also withdrew, unwilling to defend the scraps of his father’s misadventure. 
<@925830977291440210>

Upon their return from Italy, Jann left Karel to administrate the lands of the Bohemian Crown, installing him as Margrave of Moravia in 1334 and then giving him charge of the County of Tyrol in 1336 on behalf of his younger brother, **Jan Jindřich**, and his marriage to Countess **Margarete von Görz**. The most important achievement of the father-son duo between their return from Italy and his departure for crusade in Lithuania was the **Congress of Viségrad** in 1335, triggered by the death of none other than ***Heinrich von Gorizia***, whom Jann had deposed in his youth to become King of Bohemia. Heinrich’s death facilitated the Habsburg succession in Carinthia and Carniola, strengthening their position amidst their already tight commercial stranglehold over Danubian trade with the city of Wien, prompting Jann of Bohemia, Kazimierz of Poland, and Károly of Hungary to convene. The Duchy of Silesia was recognized by the Polish as ceded to Bohemia, and in return Jann abandoned his claim to the Polish throne for 1.2m Prager Groschen. The three kingdoms also agreed to cooperation in the creation of new trade routes to circumvent Wien’s domineering position.  The fate of the Tyrolean inheritance was the most recent of sparks between Haus Luxemburg and Haus Wittelsbach, with a brief conflict emerging in 1336 before being settled within the same year. The Tyrolean question remains ever persistent in the future of relations between Haus Luxembourg and Haus Wittelsbach, perhaps even moreso than Brandenburg.
-# Jan Jindřich and Margarete von Gorizia.

So who then, is ***Jann de Blannen***, but the man who became King of Bohemia at fourteen, through marriage and force of arms, who went so far as to imprison his wife and child in retribution of noble plots against him, who was born and raised in Paris and neglected his primary title for foreign adventurers and knightly forays, who campaigned into Northern Italy of his own volition to the preservation of imperial reach therein, who was adept at the gathering of subjects, and poor in winning their loyalty? Who is Jann, except a blind warrior-king, who perhaps had been blind to his own follies his whole life? 

<@190640828954181632> <@252560004069064705> <@763652399784919080> <@215639063502258176> <@306458253674545153> <@137101075411894272> <@385875387608989714> <@451075783088275497>

# Vom Aargau nach Wien
The Habsburg's namesake is much older than the duchy of Austria. The castle that gave the family its name, Habichtsburg, sits in the canton of Aargau above the Aare river, built around 1020. For two and a half centuries the house was a Swabian noble family of middling importance, holding lands in the Aargau, the Breisgau, and the upper Rhine, with the Forest Cantons of Schwyz, Uri, and Unterwalden under varying degrees of Habsburg lordship. Rudolf I changed the family's center of gravity during his reign towards the east. Elected King of the Romans in 1273, he defeated Otakar II of Bohemia at the Battle on the Marchfeld in 1278, took the Babenberg inheritance, and in 1282 invested his sons Albrecht and Rudolf with Austria and Styria.

What finally and ultimately broke the Habsburg's western identity was Morgarten. On a fateful November morning in 1315, Duke Leopold I of Austria, brother of Emperor Frederick the Fair, marched on Schwyz with a force of roughly two thousand men. The Confederates of Schwyz, Uri, and Unterwalden under Werner Stauffacher caught Leopold and his troops in a narrow pass alongside Lake Ägerisee, dropping logs and boulders from the slopes, butchering the cavalry like fish in a barrel. Over fifteen hundred Habsburg knights and mercenaries died in that narrow pass, with Duke Leopold barely escaping with his life. Morgarten removed the aura of hegemony over Swabia the Habsburgs had enjoyed, and in the years that followed, the family's center of gravity moved east in earnest. Frederick the Fair lost the imperial title and his liberty at Mühldorf in 1322. To make matters worse, the Forest Cantons consolidated themselves and began absorbing further communes into their League. Habsburg revenues from the Swabian and Aargau lands continued, but the political weight of the family had now shifted to the old Babenberg seat of Wien, which was originally built up around the Stephansdom and the Hofburg. Following Marchfeld, it became the new capital of Habsburg power. Albrecht II was born at Habsburg Castle in 1298, he was the last Habsburg to be born there in almost fifty years.

### Amedé the Great and Edward the Brief

*Amedé was born to Thomas of Savoy, the Count of Piedmont and began his early life not ruling, but in service of his cousin Edward Longshanks, accompanying the English King to many of his campaigns against the Welsh, proliferating himself as a competent knight and leader.*

*His fortune changed when his childless Uncle, Philip the Count of Savoy died in 1285, who in his will charged his grand-nephew Edward I of England with his inheritance, who awarded Savoy to Amedé, though his younger brother was awarded the Barony of Vaud, though sworn to his brother. This had been a pattern common in the fifty years before, with the House united despite territorial divisions.*

*In the years after, Amedé subjugated a few smaller Burgundian districts Bugey and Bresse, gaining him control of the lands around the Town of Vienne. In 1287, after a war on behalf of the Bishop of Geneva, the City and Counts of Geneva were forced to submit themselves as vassals. <@883471267506843668> During the Gascon Wars, Amedé aided his cousin Edward in the negotiations with the french Crown, even arranging the marriage between Edward I and Margaret of France, an event that would have great repercussions a few decades later. <@1106651545296195655> He was an ally of Emperor Henry VII of Luxembourg during his Italian campaigns <@261290667106369536> and in 1315 aided the Knights Hospitaller in the defense of Rhodes. <@259426601865838592> *

*He would be succeeded by his son by his first wife Sybille of Bage, Edward the Liberal, who only reigned for a brief six years, in which the feud with the Dauphins of Viennois deepened after ethey supported the Counts of Geneva in their ambitions for reverting the treaty of Annemasse. Further the Bishop of Sion broke his traditional ties with the House. Unexpectedly, Edward passed in 1329, leaving only his daughter Joan as legitimate issue.*

# <:cypflag:1505309557134725251>  The Two Kingdoms.

There are two Kingdoms in the Eastern Mediterranean whose King bows to the Bishop of Rome in reverence. Both born from the piety of men whom went to reclaim the Holy Land from the heathens who had stolen it <@874750350538727434> . Though by 1337, now only one of the two Kingdoms is as such in more than just name. King Hugh IV (and II) Lusignan, nephew of the last King of Jerusalem to actually control any land in Palestine, rests on his island Kingdom. He held two coronations for himself there, one for each throne he claimed.

<:Flag_CYP_crowned:1505317004503023656>  **Cyprus** - In 1324 King Henry II Lusignan died. Soon afterwards the first of King Hugh's coronations took place in Nicosie, the traditional capital for the island, though it was not without opposition. Hugh did not have the best claim for the throne, for his Aunts were legally more obligated to the crown than he was, but Hugh was the only male relative of his predecessor, Henry II, and so Hugh quickly found much more support for his claim than his Aunts could. With the vassals on his side, Hugh was crowned as King Hugh IV of Cyprus, in the Cathedral of Saint Sophia, in Nicosie. 

The island Kingdom was conquered from the bungling Byzantine rebel Prince Isaac Komnenos <@302128861209165824> over a century ago. Yet after Richard the Lionheart took the island, he realized that he had little means to control it whilst he set out on his holy quest to retake Jerusalem. So the island passed around the crusader world for a while until it came under the possession of the Kings of Jerusalem. During this time, Cyprus became a hub of trade that the Italian merchant states used to great effect to trade with the lucrative east. Cyprus' many natural resources also became prime export goods, like sugar and silk. The island Kingdom thus prospered, as it does now in 1337.

Cyprus however, is not filled with Franks, but Greeks. This at times has led to unrest and revolts, though they have yet not amounted to anything. The urban centers are where you will find the Roman Catholics and Frankish nobles. Faithful to feudal nobility and the piety of Catholicism.

The countryside meanwhile, is where the Greek and Eastern Orthodox peoples reside in far greater numbers than their overlords. Though the Catholic Church <@150411715757080576> owns most of the religious property on the island, the locals are steadfast in their own faith, and have shown little willingness to convert, though this has always been a matter of insignificance to the Lusignan Kings.

Amongst the court of Hugh IV, there are many vassals that swear allegiance to him, though most are without any real power and little more than noble fugitives from the fallen King of Jerusalem. Yet the most powerful noble family in the old Kingdom of Jerusalem still yet remains the most influential and powerful House in Cyprus, that of the Ibelins. The Ibelins have been connected with the crusader states for a very long time, and they have maintained their status and prestige even now. They opposed the ascension of Hugh IV's predecessor, and the King has worked hard since the start of his reign to warm the relations between the ruling dynasty and the Ibelins. 

Hugh IV's mother was an Ibelin, and so too was his first wife an Ibelin, and when she died in 1318, the King received a Papal Dispensation to marry again, and yet again he married an Ibelin, the current Queen Alice of Ibelin. Such is the power of that great noble House. Together between his two wives Hugh IV has by 1337 six children, and already a grandson. These children Hugh IV is planning to use to his benefit, to increase his ties to the Catholic realms to the west, to hopefully convince them to crusade oncemore, to finally reclaim his other Kingdom, the object of all Catholic desires...

----------
<:Jerusalem:742039125057863792> **Jerusalem** - Not long after King Hugh's first coronation, he traveled to Famagusta to receive the crown of Jerusalem. Traditionally he would've done so in Tyre, but of course all the lands in Palestine were lost to the Muslims many decades ago, and so this could not be done. It took some deliberation amongst those at the royal court to find which place on Cyprus might be the best alternative to Tyre to be crowned King of Jerusalem, and Famagusta was where the clergy and vassals deemed most appropriate. This Kingdom, as opposed to that of Cyprus, was merely nominal. The Holy Land was all lost, and thus the crown meant little but prestige and the legitimacy to retake those lands under the defunct crown.

To this end, King Hugh II of Jerusalem has worked hard to see his claim to the holy Kingdom confirmed by as much of the west as possible, for there were other claimants who contested Hugh's claim. As much as was feasible, Hugh II worked to legitimize himself as the true King of Jerusalem, and win for himself a crusade that would grant *himself* any land in Palestine that was conquered. Foremost amongst these efforts is that with the King of France <@460814348487753730> . For years Hugh II has had diplomats in the French capital, trying to win favor with the King and secure both his political support for Hugh's claim to Jerusalem, and more substantially his aid in a possible crusade for the Holy Land.

A big boon for his cause was King Hugh IV of Cyprus/Hugh II of Jerusalem having married his son and heir, Guy, to the daughter of Duke Louis I of Bourbon, one of the most prominent French nobles who yet plan a French involvement in a possible crusade. Hugh's hopes are that with the support of the Duke, he might win French support for his claim, and at last reclaim the lands that he owns in name only.

However, tensions between France and England <@1106651545296195655> continue to grow rapidly. If a war was to break out between those two great western Kingdoms, that would almost certainly kill all chance that King Hugh will have his crusade...

----------
**1337**

Even still, King Hugh of Cyprus and Jerusalem persists in his efforts. The island of Cyprus prospers, and plans continue to win support for a crusade. With as many children as the King has had, he does have potential to marry them into powerful families in the west. His heir, the titular Prince of Galilee Guy de Lusignan seems an able man, and worthy of the two Kingdoms. Though, if Guy were to meet an early end, another succession crisis might strike the island Kingdom, further destabilizing the Lusignan domain.

Though there might be some flickering hope, beyond those small successes in the past, all of Hugh's plans might still come to naught and ruin very soon in the future, and yet until that time comes, he persists. All he has is his family, one worthy crown and one worthless crown, and a wealthy island. But, with the grace of God, he might yet keep the Kingdom that he truly has, and reclaim the Kingdom he only *claims* to have...

**(End)**

-# King Hugh IV of Cyprus / King Hugh II of Jerusalem

-# Flag of the Kingdom of Cyprus and the Kingdom of Jerusalem

# Coronation of Emperor Ludwig IV in Rome (1328)

Holy Roman Emperor Henry VII of Luxembourg would pass in August of 1313, leaving the empire without a leader. His son, John of Luxembourg had been a promising candidate, but the rise of a rival candidate in Frederick 'the fair' of Habsburg would split the empire in twain, leading to an election period of fourteen months.

On October 19th, 1314 AD, four electors convened to elect **Frederick the Fair** of the Habsburgs as the new Holy Roman Emperor. <@190640828954181632> 

Rejecting this, a rival assembly was called on the 20th in Frankfurt and five electors would crown **Ludwig IV von Wittelsbach** as the Holy Roman Emperor. Although many of them supported John of Luxembourg, he was ultimately deemed to be too young. <@261290667106369536> 

The following decades would prove to be a disaster for both emperors. Ludwig IV would draw the ire of the papacy for naming himself emperor without papal approval and would be subsequently excommunicated. Frederick would be defeated in the battle of Muhldorf and would remain a captive under Ludwig IV.

Peace was eventually made in 1326, Frederick being named "king of Germany" while Ludwig IV was to be crowned emperor in Rome.

Ludwig IV marched to Italy soon after, undergoing his Italienzug. In January of 1328, Ludwig IV would be met with cheers in Rome as he entered the city. He would be crowned by senator Sciarra Colonna in the St. Peter's Basilica, the coronation itself witnessed by syndics and bishops of Rome.

Despite never being officially recognized nor crowned by the Pope, Ludwig IV would rule legitimately for nearly a decade, his rival and co-king Frederick having died in 1330. Such has lead to a tense and controversial idea— that an emperor may rule even without Papal approval. Even now, the emperor remains excommunicated and unrecognized by the papacy... <@150411715757080576>

# **The Duchy of Lorraine: Heirs to Lothair’s Legacy**

After the death of Louis the Pious, who had inherited the Frankish Empire from his father Charlemagne, the realm was divided among his sons into three separate kingdoms. Lothair I received the Kingdom of Lotharingia, a vast territory stretching between West and East Francia, from the North Sea all the way to Lombardy.

Through further inheritance and division, this kingdom was later separated into Lower and Upper Lotharingia. Upper Lotharingia would eventually become the Duchy of Lorraine as we know it today, though over time it too lost territories. Among these were lands in the Pays Messin, as well as the regions of Bar and Verdun, territories once ruled under the authority of the House of d’Alsace.

The House of d’Alsace was appointed as the rightful ducal dynasty by the Holy Roman Emperor Henry III due to its loyalty and strong ties to the imperial court. For nearly three centuries now, our house has ruled over the Duchy of Lorraine.

Now, however, the time has come for a young nobleman to take his place: Raoul the Bold d’Alsace, an ambitious and determined ruler. From an early age, he was raised with discipline and became known as a courageous warrior. His noble parents told him stories of Lorraine’s former greatness and power, instilling in him both pride and ambition for his homeland. One day, he hopes to restore Lorraine from obscurity and see it rise again as a great power, reclaiming the legacy of Lothair I and even Charlemagne himself.

Yet today, the duchy faces a different challenge: its position as a crossroads between the Holy Roman Empire and the Kingdom of France. Culturally and linguistically, Lorraine leans closer to France, yet diplomatically and legally it remains a member of the Holy Roman Empire and imperial territory. Thus, only time will tell where Lorraine’s destiny truly lies — whether it shall belong to one realm, or perhaps rise once more as a kingdom of its own.

Long live Lotharingia.

In 1329, the Holy Roman emperor Ludwig IV met with the sons of his brother— Ruprecht and Rudo
events.fullText:
### Treaty of Pavia (1329)
In 1329, the Holy Roman emperor Ludwig IV met with the sons of his brother— Ruprecht and Rudolf II to mend old wounds and broker peace between the Palatine and Bavarian lines of Wittelsbach. The two lines had fostered conflict as Ludwig and his brother Rudolf I were fierce rivals, the younger Rudolf I often opposing Ludwig's attempts at becoming the emperor. When Rudolf I died in England and Ludwig IV was named emperor, the conflict was to be ceased immediately.

The brothers and the emperor met in Pavia, agreeing to such terms:

- The Palatinate shall be given to Rudolf II and Ruprecht I, recognizing their claims inherited from their father.
- Agreeing to 'Erbrecht', a succession agreement that stipulated that **one branch may inherit** if the **other died out**.
- Alternation of the Electorate vote, changing with each emperor.

With that, peace was achieved. It has stood stable for eight years now. 

The Bavarian line has gained stability and trust of the Palatinate with such a maneuver, bringing peace to the two lines. The Palatine line received much prestige and influence with the title of Elector Palatine and the house seniority that comes with it. Both lines are seemingly content with the status quo.

-# *["The Treaty of Pavia which divided the House of Wittelsbach into two branches, was signed in Pavia in 1329."](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hausvertrag_von_Pavia)*

-# *"Under the accord, Emperor Louis IV granted the Electorate of the Palatinate (including the Upper Palatinate) to his older brother Duke Rudolph's descendants, Rudolph II, Rupert I and Rupert II. Louis himself kept Upper Bavaria (Oberbayern) and inherited also Lower Bavaria in 1340. **It has been agreed with the Treaty of Pavia that with the extinction of one of the branches, the other branch would inherit their possessions**."*

events.fullText:
***La Battaglia di Ferrara***

It is true, the imperial Ghibellines had brought our great house to the brink. The brothers Obizzo, Niccolo and Rinaldo D'Este rule the Marquisiate of Ferrara not because of the stable nature of our house, fractured by its many infamous actions made by Azzo Viii D'Este that had excommunicated our house, but because of the victory made by King Robert and the Guelph faction on April 14th, 1333. 

King John of Luxembourg, along with Ghibelline aligned anti-papal legates of Pope John XXII, had commenced a great siege on the city of Ferrara, along with its D'Este lords. In a two month affair, the siege was in desperate need of relief. As their aim was for Ferrara to flip its allegiance away from the Guelph cause. King Robert De'Anjou had come to relieve the city from the massive papal army, and with a sortie made by Rinaldo D'Este it had squashed the attackers working beneath the cities walls. It was a full proof success for the D'Este of Ferrara, only made by the saving grace by King Robert. 

Only had this battle reinvigorated Pope John XXII to improve our standing, lifting the families excommunication and making the D'Este Papal Vicariates of Ferrara. The D'Este brothers had vocally been against the Anti Pope in Avignon as well, keeping to honoring Rome. 

Only now the Veronese Della Scala put a wrench into affairs in northern Italy, now siding with no one and taking all that they can from neighboring states. Whether the Neapolitans take to worry about such a matter is not known to Obizzo III and his brother Niccolo. 

The recent acquisition of Modena by Niccolo D'Este marks a new era for our family, sanctioned by Pope John XXII to become Communal leaders of the city. Integrating its people, culture and dynastical estates is going to prove a challenge in the coming years, and a time of neutrality will be necessary for the D'Este to enforce its rule and apply Estesian customs.

## Mainz and Trier, a brewing rivalry

By 1337, Baldwin has held the seat of Archbishop of Trier for 30 years. Baldwin while a Noble and appointed based more so on connections than simple merit has not been an absentee Bishop. His family's seat of Luxembourg being so close to Trier must be considered in part of that reason. But Baldwin has by no means been a devoted Archbishop to his diocese. The office of Archbishop or Trier that Baldwin holds has been used and will be used to further the ambitions of the Luxembourg imperial Dynasty.

In 1328 Baldwin and the Mainz cathedral chapter defied the Pope and appointed Baldwin as the administrator of the vacant Archbishopric of Mainz. Baldwin was able to use his local prestige as the Archbishopric of Trier and a memeber of the house of Luxembourg to act as administrator until (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_bishops_and_archbishops_of_Mainz#Archbishops_and_Prince-Electors,_1251–1803). Much to the annoyance of the Papal appointed Heinrch III von Virneberg who was only recently able to usurp the role of administrator from Baldwin with the backing of the Avignon Pope.

The recent loss of the more prestigious and more prosperous Archbishopric of Mainz due to the involvement of the Papacy is a defining moment between the relationship of the Archbishopric of Trier, The Archbishopric of Mainz and the Avignon Papacy.

Without proper compensation for his lost income as the Administrator of Mainz, Baldwin will likely develop a personal **rivalry **with the Archbishop of Mainz and the upper clergy of Trier will be sure to follow the lead of their Archbishop of 30 years.

events.fullText:
# 1336: the Year of the latest Failure.

After the loss of Acre in 1291, which ended the crusader presence in Palestine, the Council of Vienne in 1311 - 1312 set up the foundations for a renewed effort at retaking the Holy Land. Philip IV of France <@460814348487753730>  took the cross, as did his son-in-law, Edward II of England <@1106651545296195655> . Though such an act meant less and less as the centuries since the first crusade occurred, it was still a promising sign for King Henry II of Cyprus, but disaster would soon strike this planned crusade. Philip IV's death in 1314, the same year as Pope Clement V <@150411715757080576> , dealt a harsh blow to the effort. That, combined with Edward II's defeat at Bannockburn in 1314, and a Europe-wide famine from 1315 - 1317 killed any hope of the Vienne crusade. Though King Charles IV of France would in 1323 sponsor a flotilla for use in the east, his death just five years later meant that the fleet would never sail towards the Holy Land.

However, King Philip VI of France would be heavily engaged in negotiating, planning, and preparing for a new Holy Land expedition throughout the years between 1331 - 1336. Philip would even take the cross in 1333 after having secured Papal appointment as the Catholic Church's "Rector and Captain-General" and receiving great financial aid from Pope John XXII. King Hugh IV of Cyprus was delighted to work with yet another French King towards a crusade for the Holy Land, and he eagerly and vigorously supported the French efforts, even securing the personal support of the prominent Duke Louis of Bourbon with a marriage arrangement between Hugh's heir and Louis' daughter.

All seemed to be going well, and the French King even sponsored efforts to reduce the Turkish pirate presence in the Aegean, but the realities of European politics were soon to rear its ugly head at Hugh IV's hopes.

The fact of the matter was, Philip VI would need to secure his frontiers before he could set out for Cyprus, and these frontiers were only growing more and more unstable by the year. Italy was in chaos, disputes between the French King, Papacy, and Holy Roman Emperor still unresolved. The Iberian Kingdoms cared little to help in the crusading effort, and England was desperately trying to oust Philip's ally David Bruce of Scotland. Pope Benedict XII was growing suspicious of Philip's diverting of crusade taxes, and events years later in 1337 would only go to further show just how little the Papacy and French King could cooperate. And, to truly kill off all ideas of crusading, Philip soon realized that raising the necessary men, money, and materials for the crusade was much harder than he anticipated. 

All these concerns worsened and melded until, finally, Philip VI had enough, and Pope Benedict XII cancelled the crusade in **1336**. The French fleet which was going to be used in the crusade was instead sent to the English Channel in anticipation of conflict with the English, and King Hugh IV was left with his hopes dashed.

The failure to crusade despite all the efforts made to do so has not insignificantly damaged the image of the French Monarchy, probably leading to the shocking Papal actions against Philip VI in 1337. King Hugh IV of Cyprus meanwhile is left without many options. Hugh IV's entire foreign policy revolved around gaining French support for a crusade, and having come so close to his goal only to watch its rapid collapse, he doesn't seem to have any other ideas on how to accomplish the enormous task of retaking the Holy Land. Unable to find another solution, Hugh IV is stuck in inaction, his diplomats in Paris now merely bumbling around, continuing to try to revive Philip VI's old crusading mentality, though even to the diplomats it is obvious that this is futile and a waste of time.

(Rp Context: To represent this stagnate foreign policy in regards to the failure of 1336, I will be sending an in-game diplomat to improve relations with France until either Hugh IV dies or relations are improved to the max, which will waste my in-game diplo resources obviously.)

**1336** was a fatal blow to the ambitions of Hugh IV, and it appears that it will be left to his heir to figure out a new policy in regards to how to organize a crusade for the Holy Land. At the very least, Hugh IV has many children he could marry off, which could win him support amongst the other Kingdoms of Christendom. If the reaction of both embarrassment and rage across Europe at the failure of 1336 is anything to go by, the crusading spirit is not fully dead just yet, and in the decades ahead there could be another effort aimed against the Kingdom of Cyprus' main Muslim adversary. However, that would likely require a period of calm in Europe, and a new King on Cyprus, one young and bold and full of religious fervor. Perhaps that time will come. Or...perhaps it won't. None could say, but the failure of 1336 will doubtlessly continue to haunt the Lusignan Kings of Cyprus and Jerusalem for decades to come.

**(End)**

-# Council of Vienne (1331 - 1332)

## The Moselle, nestled between the Eiffel and the Hunsrück 

The Diocese of Trier, the land, and its people are nestled on the Moselle valley. It is in so many ways a forgotten part of Europe. The Moselle valley is poorer and much more sparsely populated than the Rhine valley to the east or the fertile French plains that lie westward through the vast Ardennes. The **locations ** of the Moselle and the Diocese of Trier average 10,000 souls each, **the town of Trier** hold but a mere 18,000 souls. To the east, the lands of the Rhine average roughly 20,000 souls to location. **The City of Mainz** overshadows **the Town of Trier** with its 45,000 souls. While in France a staggering 30,000 souls live on average in each Location. 
The lands of the Moselle are dominated by the rolling hills of the Eiffel to the North and the Hunsrück to the south. The Diocese is dominated by Woods and by thick, dense Forests that hold little economical value and as mentioned hold even fewer people. The Moselle river often bypassed by traders and merchants, whom prefer to take the north south route of the Rhone up to the Rhine, passing by Mainz and Koln, bypassing Trier entirely.
Both the Rhine valley and the French plains overshadow the Moselle valley in almost every economic regard.

[Date:1337-01-01][Country:TRI][Location:Trier][Tag:Economy ]
## The Moselle, nestled between the Eiffel and the Hunsrück, pt 2

The difficulties that this land presents have lead to the Lay Clergy, the Commoners and the few Burghers to be a **deeply spiritualist people**. And a people that value Community over the individual. These two values are in contrast to that of the typical Archbishop. The Archbishop almost always a second son of a Noble is often appointed either due to nepotism, or outright simony. And the noble Archbishop knowing the poverty of the land, more so looking for the prestige that the title of Archbishop than he duty for the souls of his diocese is often an **absentee** Archbishop. The Noble Archbishop seeks to use Trier to further his own goals, of his Dynastic family, not the simple spiritual wellbeing of Trier.

events.fullText:
**A Rising Princedom**

It is known that the gorod (fort) that formed Muscovy started taking proeminence when Deniyel of Moskva took power within 1263, receiving the least important lands from his father Aleksandr Nevsky at the age of 2 years old as an otchina, where the lands would be governed by his uncle Yaroslav until 1282. The gorod was a small military outpost noteworthy for being sacked by the mongols in 1238.

This would change, however, as Deniyel would win in a war against his brothers and unify the lands of his father under the capital of Moskva, forming the principality of Moskva, under the Grand Principality of Vladimir.

In one of his last acts, Deniyel defeated Ryazan, taking their northern lands, in 1301, increasing the territory of the principality to almost all of the Moskva river and its principalities.

Deniyel's son, Yury, would find himself in conflict with Tver, a conflict that would be on and off and last over 30 years due to inheritance issues against the Prince of Tver, Mikhail. Seeking approval from the Khan of the Golden Horde, he was also elected Prince of Novgorod in 1307. He would eventually be murdered in 1325 by Mikhail's son Dmitry in Sarai.

Ivan I is the third and current ruler of Moskva, having, through diplomatic and military manuevering, consolidate more territory and been elected Grand Prince of Moskva and Vladimir, as well as prince of Novgorod, in approval with the Khan. Conflicts once again arose in Tver over the murder of his father and the succession, though no military action was taken by intervention of Lithuania.

Moskva has had a short but rising history, and as Grand Prince of the Rus, has the potential to rise even further.

events.fullText:
# Primas Germaniae

At the bureaucratical center of the archbishopric of Mainz, lies its resplendent namesake: The free city of Mainz.

The city itself is positively ancient, with its founding reaching back all the way to when the romans ruled over much of southern Germany. Its conversion to the religion of Christianity was also rather fast, and already in the year of 700 it was declared an official archbishopric.

Alongside the other clerical electors of Trier and Köln, the archbishopric of Mainz holds an important title within the holy roman empire and act as a living extension and enforcer of the pope’s will (and by extension, the puppeteers controlling the “big papa” behind the scenes.)

As one of the most populous cities, Mainz has been granted the prestigious Archdiocese of Germany. While Trier and Köln have to deal with non-german congregations (the archdiocese of Burgundy and Italy respectively) Mainz holds the honour of ensuring that all in the holy roman empire are true and pious Christians.

As an extension of this, the archdiocese stretches over the largest swathes of imperial land compared to all the other dioceses, with its members counting many princes, cities and of course Bishoprics and abbeys. The relevant clerical states being:

Paderborn
Corvey
Verden
Halberstadt
Hildesheim
Hersfeld
Würzburg
Fulda
Eichstätt
Augusta Vindelicorum
Chur
Disentis
Strasbourgum
Spira

-# Archdiocese of Mainz

-# And with the relevant clerical states highlighted in green.

These all fall under the direct responsibility of the Archbishoprich of Mainz, a responsibility that they - as the prime voice of the pope north of the alps - **take very seriously**. Though still, in an age of increasing decadence and clerical corruption, there are few cases that cannot be guided along with a few different gifts of gold.

**[This is to say: All conquest/vassalisation of any of the aforementioned states, *will* incur meddling from Mainz, either in the form of economic sanctions, spiritual sanctions, diplomatic sanctions, complete and active military aid or all those together.**

**UNLESS permission is given from Mainz first, permission that can very easily be guided along with a bit of mammon 🫰 💰 .]**

<@261290667106369536> <@792904515795550218> <@150411715757080576> <@288410456526422016> <@190640828954181632> <@564196329132261376> <@137101075411894272> <@112960565344804864> <@420614608399761419> <@883471267506843668> <@385875387608989714> <@763652399784919080><@990811778474717205> <@1178339172537339909><@222439858927435776>

# First among equals

Alongside holding the archdiocese of Germany, Mainz also holds the equally impressive titles of Archchancellor of the empire and Primas Germinae - first of the germans. WHile both titles are largely ceremonial, they still grant some power that the bishop is more than happy to make use of.

With their titles Mainz gets the honour of being the prime representative of the Pope in Germany, and accordingly all manners that are conducted between the pope and German princes are expected to go through Mainz directly or at the very least letting the Archbishoprich be present for the conversation. 

Secondly, their office of archchancellor gives the archbishoprich the honoured duty of calling imperial elections *and* delivering the first vote on the death of an emperor. Though that in itself gives little actual power, it lends loads of influence upon the bishopric and few would wish for its conquest with that important of a task. 

In this age of decadence and corruption, a few dissident voices have begun to rung out but they are still surely silenced. Yes, the current archbishop is little more than a second noble’s son, bribed to get a powerful seat and yes, the insides of the church are plated with gold while the people outside labour in the mud, but there is still little to deny that this is all the will of God.

And the will of God ought to be obeyed.

events.fullText:
**Ivan I and Novgorod**

Despite being elected prince of Novgorod by the Veche the relationship between the Grand Prince of Rus and the merchant city and the Veche is... complicated.

Tver stands between Moskva and Novgorod, and the presence of Aleksandr of Tver is troublesome, as the son of his father's murderer, the family feud lives on, despite Mikhail's execution by the Khan. The succession crisis that was sparked with his father Yury does not help matters.

Nevertheless, Novgorod remains very autonomous, as even though Ivan I, in his role as collector of tribute for the Khan, demands payments from the Veche, they are left to their own devices most of the time. Recently, in 1329, Pskov recognized Aleksandr as their prince, and Ivan marched together with Novgorodian forces to punish the princedom for their treason, ending up with Pskov as a semi-autonomous princedom under Novgorod, while Aleksandr escaped to Lithuania, where he would die in 1331.

Ivan I was then made Grand Prince, for loyalty to the Khan and helping them resolve issues. However, troubles arised when Khan Ozbeg to increase tribute, seeing Ivan as someone willing to extract more taxes from the princes. Without a mean to defy the Khan, he was forced to do so, however the Veche of Novgorod refused. Ivan sought to punish the Veche and removed his governors there, sacking the lands surrounding the city but leaving it and its armies untouched, bringing the tribute to the Khan.

A threat of invasion by Sweden and border skirmishes along the Karelian border forced the two to work together again, where they repelled the assault and reconcilled. However when the Khan once again demanded more tribute and Novgorod refused, Ivan got angry, though relations once again calmed down thanks to Metropolitan Theognostus in 1334 and a mutual threat by the Lithuanians over the Novgorodians breaking a treaty to favor Moskva.

events.fullText:
**The Tartar Yoke**

When the Mongols invaded within the decades of 1220, Russia had little choice, as their armies were defeated and cities sacked, barring Novgorod, to submit to the Khan, and their successors.

For over 100 years Russia has lived under the Tartar Yoke, with the Grand Prince being responsible for collecting tribute to give to Sarai, a role recently passed to Moskva and Ivan I, who was viewed by the Khan as the ideal servant.

This perception has made the Khan increase the amount of tributes demanded, something that has caused anger within the Russian people, and in secret, Ivan himself.

While the Mongol Empire disintegrated, and tensions arise in the east, the Golden Horde still remains strong, but Russia looks for signs of weakness and a chance to break free from this yoke.

**A New Sprout in The Garden of Balkans**

Before the rule of Basarab over Wal
events.fullText:
[Date:1337-01-01][Country:Wallachia][Location:Targoviste][Tag:History]

**A New Sprout in The Garden of Balkans**

Before the rule of Basarab over Wallachia, Vlach people and lands laid beneath the shadow of the Hungarian crown, whose court had been filled with Cumans and Latin lords. Vlach voivodes ruled in the lands of Transylvania and Wallachia and as the tribute demanded from them grew, so too did their discontent toward Hungarian rule. 
Litvoi, whose power and influence had grown in the regions of Wallachia and Transylvania, refused to pay the demanded tribute and in the end war broke out between him and the crown. Litvoi perished in his war in battle. Litvoi’s endeavor failed, but not in vain as his actions created cracks in Hungarian rule over the Vlach people. 

With Hungarian rule withering over Vlach voivodes, this paved the path for unification of Wallachian lands under one’s rule. Within the cracks of the Hungarian crown rose a Vlach voivode who united plains of Wallachia under his rule, the voivode was Basarab first of his name and house. 
With his power and influence over the lands he refused to grant land and pay tribute to the Crown and within this conflict war broke out with the Hungarian King invading his subjects' land. Hungarians lost the war after being defeated in the battle of Posada, which in the end gave way to Wallachian independence from the crown.

Wallachia’s release from the Hungarian crown was the first victory, but the peace is yet to be seen, as Transylvania and its Vlach people remained under the Hungarian crown. Though the burden of tribute had been lifted, a new danger arose from the East, and without Hungarian protection we stood exposed to the Mongol threat, and we must now defend our lands with our own strength and perhaps even lend aid to our kin in Moldavian plains, if the opportunity arises.

…

7 years have passed since the momentous day that Wallachia got its freedom from Hungarian rule, as we still stand strong and free, gaining more recognition over our rule of Wallachian land and being a sovereign state and not Hungarian subject, but still Hungary looms over land and people of Wallachia waiting.
But our efforts seem to be not in vain, as from the horizon a messenger approaches bearing the banners of Constantinople towards Targoviste.

events.fullText:
The kontor in Cologne is quite a sight. The city has privileges regarding the trade going upstream, and thus it is a buoyant city full of opportunities, with the kontor being one of the main hubs. Inside the walls, the Archbishop is nowhere to be seen, which for many means prosperity is ensured, and no doubt the aldermen in the kontor wishes that it remains like this.

The aldermen, Adolph, does not need to heed the Hansa's every whim; the league rarely works like this, even if Lübeck would want it. So, when a representative of the Church asks for a favor, Adolph takes the initiative instead of forwarding any message and asks for a favor back <:Price_expensive:1345840629258780703> 

||"I completely understand. Everyone makes mistakes, and it is a terrible misfortune to have one’s name sullied because of them. Thankfully, it is not in the spirit of a merchant to judge or impede one’s desires; if anything, we provide to everyone, everywhere. Within bounds, of course. 

It is then regrettable that in many cases, princes oppose this oh so generous wish of ours! See, when I was younger I attempted my trade in Italy, but it was clear that the merchants there will never allow me or other germans to conduct business properly. They use their influence on italian princes to forbid our trade, or make us run at losses. We would need a patron, someone who allows us to build our first outposts in Italy to secure recognition.

It is quite a pickle, that the desires on some and the desires of others lead us to these crossroads. In any case, I believe it may be possible, thanks to our generous nature as traders, to see that this understandable incident is barely talked about, if at all, in our circles. We’ll have to see”||

events.snippet: In Lübeck, the bürgermeister Hermann Monrneweg, the closest there is to a leader in the (for now) informal Hanseatic Lea
events.fullText:
In Lübeck, the bürgermeister Hermann Monrneweg, the closest there is to a leader in the (for now) informal Hanseatic League, is devising a plan to counteract the inevitable break in hostilities between the french royal families in England and France. A great point of contention will no doubt be Flanders, perhaps the richest area in Europe. Because the Hansa has backed England and will continue to do so, there is a chance Flanders ends up out of reach of the Hansa traders. Should the Kontor in Brugge be closed, the Hansa needs another way to move wool, dyes and cloth <:Burghers:1139045024890368001> 

So, while drinking some mead, he puts aside the plans for the northern Baltic expansion through Visby and the Peterhof. He’ll come back later to those; it’s clear that the Hansa ought to focus on what they are best… And Count Guillaume III seems a reasonable man. When word reaches Monrneweg about his good disposition, he advises the aldermen from Brugge to meet with him. After all, any Kontor or office in Holland will be overseen from Brugge (or become the main hub if worse comes to pass…)

||When he arrives, he brings some gifts to present Guillaume, mostly artisanry found in Flanders, and some from England, including a great cloth embroidered with his coat of arms. Nothing less for such an important person (*I’ll send 10 gold, remind me at game start if I forget*), and nothing less to ameliorate the deal. 

The deal offered is simple: allowing the Hansa to operate within the cities of Holland, as they do in Flanders. First with trade offices, and if the Count believes it works, then perhaps a Kontor can be established. Hansa membership is another matter that would require more serious discussion, and it would depend on the evolving politics in Brugge, but the benefits are always tangible for rulers! More trade always means more tariffs, more tolls, and more prestige for the local artisans||

** The Second Breton Succession Crisis  **

It is 1337, John the III son of Arthur II, Duke of Brittany **whose mother was Beatrice of England daughter to the Plantagenet king Henry the III** and Marie, Viscountess of Limoges who brought the county of Limoges under Breton control, has ruled for 25 good years, he is loved, he is good. A friend of the current French king <@460814348487753730>  Phillip the VI and closely related to multiple of the Valois at court has led to his duchy enjoying autonomy and power, his marriage to Joan of Savoy <@564196329132261376>  only child of Edward, Count of Savoy giving him claim to Savoy, while also keeping ties with Welsh princedoms, Scotland <@1417260821674852363>  and even England <@1106651545296195655> , retaining the old title of earl of Richmond a title held by the Breton nobility since the days of 1066. Yet for all of this he is also childless, the simple truth now being that if he was going to have an heir he would have by now, so comes the hard question as always, who is to succeed? There are two factions in contention, his closest male relative John de Montfort, half-brother and his closest relative his niece, Joan de Penthièvre, **Gods preserve us. **

**Montfort's** 

John de Montfort is half-brother to the current duke by means of Arthur the II’s second marriage to Yolande of Dreux who was **widow of Alexander the III of Scotland** and held the titles of Montfort-l'Amaury which passed to him upon her death. Granting him control over fertile salt beds in Loire Valley and strategic castle of Montfort that guards the western approaches of Paris. However he is hated by his half-brother who resented his father's second marriage and the amount of wealth and power they held, upon his father's death he had attempted to strip them of all resources and influence in Brittany, even going as far as to petition the **Pope **to annul his father's second marriage and bastardise his half brother in the process. 

An amicable and friendly man ironically much like his half-brother he lacks in political wisdom or sharpness meaning he was unable to do much to counter his half sibling pushing him out of the realm. Despite Salic law being on his side he lacks supporters in Brittany and his brothers hatred of him is well known, to the point that John even attempted to directly hand the Duchy to the king of France rather than risk him having it. Though John is not alone for his **wife Joan of Flanders** supports his claim with fiery ambition, backing him with her own personal fortune from Flanders, Nevers and Rethel along with advising him (*many in fact believing that she is in fact masterminded of much of his political camp)*.

Though he has made little attempt directly to request outsider support, believing that the French king will back his claim in keeping with Salic law others in his faction are not so foolish to think that Philip VI is motivated by law alone. Through his wife wealth and mercantile focus and his own relationship with **Scottish lords of Murray and the Isles he could become a focus for Scottish and Genoese <@170593650584059904>  mercenaries**. While in England it is known well he is the outsider in Breton politics and if given the right incentives *(or his wife instead)* could well see the utility in swearing to **Edward the III** if the French crown will not give him justice.

**Blois** 

Joan de Penthièvre is the niece of the current duke, daughter of Guy de Penthièvre and his wife Jeanne d'Avaugour, whose territories passed to Joan giving her control over northwestern Brittany through owning the port town of Sant-Brieg. When the truth that the Duke John would not be having children nor would he be allowed by his own Parliament to effectively give the state away to the French king it became apparent that whoever married Joan would in effect come to rule Brittany.** Marriage offers from Edward the III in the form of his younger brother, the king Alfonso XI of Castile and numerous French lords**. In the end Charles de Blois-Châtillon **nephew to king Philip VI **would take her hand. This match in relation to the king of France combined with the issue in all likelihood going to the parliament in Paris has effectively set the Blois faction as the **front runner **despite the fact itself does not conform to French salic law.  

Joan has spent most of her life in eastern Brittany, independently spirited and some would say even spiteful her political forwardness and self-assuredness at her own stake has meant her camp has remained confident in its victory. Coupled with this her husband who would become duke via the customary law of right of position in the given legal context is a** strongly pious man, whose devotion has been known to border on fanatic**, despite that he is an experienced knight, intelligent and charismatic along with being surprisingly supportive of Breton culture despite being French himself,** having made even a holy oath to the Duke that he will uphold the independence of the duchy on his life**. Their faction remains in the majority for now, however it is said overconfidence is a slow and insidious killer.....

Charles relationship to the king of France means the faction is **confident of support** they could get from the crown of France, though in truth with the realm stretched as it is they may end up being sourly mistaken. Charles devotion to the church <@150411715757080576> has meant most expect the bishop of Tours (*which has domain over Brittany)* and by extension the papacy to be more amenable to their claim if it brought to the church. While Joans more traditionalist outlook could even see her render support from **Wales or Cornwall**.

events.fullText:
Ivan I "Kalita", Grand Prince of Moskva and the Rus, has been elected prince of Novgorod by the Veche in 1332, 5 years ago, in reality, despite appointing some governors to handle the tribute collected to go to the Khan Ozbeg, Novgorod enjoys much autonomy, being the center of trade within the north, however that also welcomes issues. The Lithuanians, Teutons and Swedish have ambitions over their land, and Muscovite troops have been sent before to guarantee their safety against common foes.

Ivan I Rurikovich makes his way towards the Veche, accompanied by Archbishop Gediminas of Moskva, something he has not done ever since his coronation as prince of Novgorod, in order to discuss their agreements, as well as a few proposals.

Firstly, he reinforces the guarantee of security done by Muscovy against foreign threats, their rights to the princedom of Pskov, which had sided with Tver and Lithuania a few years earlier and been given to them as a reward for their help.

Secondly, he offers a proposal, he seeks to destroy the threat of Tver and Prince Konstantin the son of the usurper and his father's murderer, Tver being by right of succession his. This, Moskva can do own its own, however doing so would secure a land connection to Novgorod. Moskva wishes to then assist Novgorod in taking the territory of Beloozero, who has assisted Tver in the past, as well as support Novgorodian efforts in contest the Swedish in the Finnish wild, potentially taking the fortress of Viborg, in exchange for them handling trade of Muscovite iron and vodka through the baltics.

events.fullText:
## The Fehden, The Feud

**Our **right to settle differences, settle personal enmity and settle that of dishonour is the law of the land, it is the customary law.

In the German lands, there is no central, overbearing King to ensure a general peace. Merely a weak and ineffectual Emperor whom had to start [his reign at war with a rival house](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545415213449216/1506090837736554637), merely to make to claim the crown as his. So by what right are we as his subjects any different? He who is meant to be our temporal lord, above all just and fair. Settles his differences and his grievances with **us** by war. Then why should we not settle our differences among ourselves by war? **Is war simply not the law of these lands?**

So we as Germans, as subjects of the Holy Roman Emperor **shall settle our internal differences by war.**

The armies raised are small, but mobile. There goal is not siege their adversaries strongholds, nor to defeat them in open battle. A prolonged siege presents the every present risk of disease, dysentery the primary fear. And there is danger and risk to life in fighting another Knight fully clad in Armour and on Horseback.  No the primary primary outlet for venting frustration on a neighbour was the burning of the neighbours villages and the killing of their serfs. Not only is it safer for the feuding nobility to vent their anger on an unarmed peasant, but it also presents "economic frustrations" for the neighbouring lord. It is nye on impossible for a lord to ~~exploit~~ tax a presents when the presents homes and fields are burnt and what little livestock they have are slaughtered. The burning of fields and murdering of serfs might just "frustrate" the neighbouring lord just enough that he might seek a treaty to end the feud. 

-# Further more nuanced reading than the above: https://library.fes.de/libalt/journals/swetsfulltext/16590476.pdf

**The Orsini Rise to Power <:rose:865579087883272192> **

The House of Orsini traces its influence back to as early as 752, when one of its own ascended to the Papacy as Pope Stephen II. Through his reign, Stephen seized the opportunity to elevate his house into one of prestige, influence, and enduring power. In the centuries since, the Orsini have produced three Popes and countless cardinals, quietly guiding the currents of the Curia from behind the veil.

The Orsini see themselves as civilization itself, guardians of the Church, a necessary evil in the preservation of God’s glory. For without strong hands to guide Christendom, there is only chaos, as history has shown. The fall of Rome, the endless intrigues of the Church, and the exploitation of the Papacy by foreign crowns, particularly the French monarchy, are not righteous acts. There must be defenders of the faith, protectors of order against ambition and decay. Such has long been the role of the Black Nobility, and among them, none stand greater than House Orsini.

Over the last hundred years, the Orsini have risen to dominate the Curia almost entirely, wielding immense influence over Papal elections and the appointment of cardinals, even those expected to answer to the King of France. Through diplomacy, wealth, intimidation, and political mastery, Napoleo Orsini has worked tirelessly to uproot French influence from the Church. The French King watches the rise of the Black Nobility with growing unease, knowing well that Rome no longer bends so easily to foreign crowns.

Napoleo Orsini rules Rome. He commands the Church. He sees himself not merely as a prince of the Curia, but as an Augustus Caesar reborn, destined to restore stability to a fractured Christendom.

**Ave Orsini!**

events.fullText:
# The Signora of Milan

Nearly thirty years ago, the Visconti expelled the della Torre and established themselves as the true rulers of Milan.

The neighbouring cities of Lodi, Bergamo, Novara, and Cremona, long used as power bases by the della Torre, have since been absorbed into Milanese control. The Visconti also claim imperial favor when they purchased the title of Imperial Vicar of Milan from the Holy Roman Empire in 1327.

Yet Milan remains divided. Beneath Visconti rule, the city stands at a crossroads. The old noble families still resent the rise of one ruling house, while della Torre seek allies among Guelph sympathizers, both internally and externally in hopes of restoring their influence.

To the east, the Scala of Verona have built a domain stretching across northern Italy from the Adriatic toward the Tyrrhenian. Their growing power now threatens to contest Milan’s claim over Lombardy itself.

If the Visconti are to endure, they must transform Milan from a city into a centralized state. Milan must integrate the cities under its dominion, secure imperial legitimacy, and drive back the della Scala before they can dominate northern Italy.

events.fullText:
# A TO A AS B IS TO B, C IS TO SEE, DON'T YOU SEE?
## AND SO THE PARADE BEGINS

The freaks assemble in their dozens marching to the tune of a limestone tomb. Baring wigs, rods, musical instruments, ornate daggers, enamel baskets, sticks of terracotta, their bodies contorting into all manner of stone-burdened forms along the trapezoid trapeze boulevard lined with carroway weeds fragrant with corpse-exhumed bloodied blood-soaked dreams. And so the procession continues along the rythm of funerary rites by the dirge of a thousand deltas, merging and splitting and following the bent oxbows, wet, warm, sticky, hot, overflooding, weeds reeds and cruel un-mercies inflicted onto the river banks hence reaching the sea and ocean: where, finding naught but the endless scorching overcast sun and they were tightly wrapped in bandages, suffocating, our mad marcher's methods had no recourse but to continue and stepped foot first and foremost into the water, sinking, drowning.

Having made their beds in atavistic tombs of iron and bearing the brands of a slave-race punished, our dead pall-bearers had nothing much in the way about anything. There's was a tale repeated, circular, bound upon a wheel of transparent repetition, looped around on itself: ouroboros, Ra-Osiris, Siriso-Ar, soroboruo: flesti no dnuora depool, noititeper tnerapsnart fo leehw a nopu dnuob, ralucric, detaeper elat a saw s'ereht! Palindromically, the laid bricks upon themselves reinforced with a sturdy form, to the sky pointing, thricefold, their calendric slopes did not beguile me. But our march continues.

# Ägypten!

Where the sleeping dogs lie!

And let them lie.

events.fullText:
**Petershof**

The Hanseatic Kontor on the right bank of the Volkhov, even with the sometimes unsteady relations between the republic and the league. still remains as one of the largest German trading posts in the East.  Consisting of a palisaded enclave further up hull from the river, and a more public and local beachyard front where ships and trade could flow out into the Baltic sea. 

A place of nebulous legal authority like any other Kontor, the Peterhof found its allegiance and loyalty to the city of Visby within the league, though a fact that is contested by some other members such as the city of Lubeck. With the consolidation of the separate Hansa's into the league as a whole. the influence of the league and its traders found themselves dominant over their other outside trading rivals such as the scandinavian powers.

The hof's main focus was surrounded around St.Peter's church from where the Kontor's archive, coffers and other uses were often found. While the Kontor's regulations on intermixing with outsiders are more restricted than most, with Novgorodian men not allowed into the courtyard when night falls, and the distance allowing the head of the Kontor more influence than in most others. 
 
As of 1337 relations as well as the flow of white and gray fur, Beeswax and salt are traded for goods such as herring, silver, tin and lead were good. But given the lacking need for western goods, silver oft flowed out into Russia and further east due to the imbalance in trade, though of course not without netting the German merchants plenty of profit.
