The Wettins who rule so proudly in Meissen, holding the title of Margrave, were not under the impression—which would be false—tha
events.fullText:
# A WETTIN AMBITION

The Wettins who rule so proudly in Meissen, holding the title of Margrave, were not under the impression—which would be false—that they were, at the moment, the preeminent house in influence and renown in Saxony. However, like any house of any renown, the House of Wettin is one of ambition: this being the simple nature of politics, being content is a rarity, and an exception, and something applicable to an individual; that is to say, the *house is ambitious*.

The House of Wettin holds two titles of note, that of the previously aforementioned Meissen, and similarly the Landgraviate of Thuringia. This, undoubtedly, elevates the Wettins to the position of being a house of renown within the Empire—the one true empire in Christendom, as the pope himself is the sole arbiter of such a thing—and thus the House of Wettin has ambitions beyond its current lot, mainly, to see themselves in a position where they hold the wealth, titles, land and privileges held by the House of Ascania. The crown jewel, one could say, of the Wettin’s ambitions is the most precious thing held by the House of Ascania seated in the Duchy of Saxe-Wittenberg: the Electorate of the Saxons. It is understandable that the great houses of Saxony, following the dissolution of the old Stem Duchy of Saxony, would like to see themselves ascended to lord over Saxony, with the other houses their vassals—or gone, if such means prove a necessity.
⠀

Luckily for the House of Wettin, negotiations are in order with the House of Welf, among which is Magnus von Welf, the so called ‘Pious’, who is soon to inherit the lands of Landsberg from his wife, Sophia Askanier; the man has no wish to hold these lands, and so is offering to sell them back to the House of Wettin, who—obviously, so much so that it need not be stated!—are the rightful holders of this land. One must put out of mind the fact that the House of Wettin originally sold the lands in the first place; desperate times lead to poor decisions, ones which Friedrich II von Wettin, Margrave of Meissen, Landgrave of Thuringia, shall see corrected.

Holding the lands of Meissen, Thuringia and soon Landsberg, the House of Wettin will ascend to the highest power in Saxony, a position it will be sure to leverage to obtain the Electorate of the Saxons: through diplomacy, preferably, but other measures if necessary.

The House of Wettin also has not been slothful in making connections with the other powerful princes of the Empire, as the Wettins have forged an alliance with the House of Hesse—who rule in Hesse—and Friedrich II von Wettin, Margrave of Meissen, Landgrave of Thuringia, future Margrave of Landsberg, and—God willing—the prospective future Elector of the Saxons, or their progenitor, whatever God’s plan may entail, has also married Mathilde von Wittelsbach, daughter of the King of the Romans, and Emperor of the Holy Roman Empire, <@763652399784919080>, and sister of <@1178339172537339909>. And certainly, let it be known, the House of Wettin is no fair-weather friend, and if the Wittelsbach are good to them, the Wettins shall be good in turn—the House of Wettin aligns itself with the Wittelsbach over the Habsburgs, for certain.
⠀

If one wishes to *be ambitious*, they cannot just focus on expansion outwards, in land or influence, but also in strengthening the foundation upon which they stand. One does not build a castle upon a foundation of mud. The House of Wettin, in its endless capacity for self-reflection, is aware of its lands being most kindly described as *underdeveloped*. However, the lands of Meissen possess a treasure that is not matched by its neighbors—in the town of Freiberg is a great silver mine, one which shall form the backbone of Meissen’s economy. Friedrich II von Wettin is not the most intelligent man, though this may not be something you would hear him say out loud, but is a capable enough steward of his lands. The silver mine of Freiberg will be the foundation upon which the wealth of the House of Wettin is built.

## The Battle of San Felice

From Luxembourg, Prince Karel was summoned in 1331 by his father, *Jang der Blinnen*, to join him in his ongoing dynastic
events.fullText:
## The Battle of San Felice

From Luxembourg, Prince Karel was summoned in 1331 by his father, *Jang der Blinnen*, to join him in his ongoing dynastic project in Lombardy. He arrived on March 29 to Pavia, which alongside the cities of Brescia, Bergamo, Parma, Cremona, Reggio, Modena, and Lucca, formed the “Luxembourg Signoria.” There, Jang was prominent amongst the strife and chaos of ever-serpentine Italy. What Karel had been summoned for, was to serve as his father’s vicar over the signoria, as an education in rule. He learnt lessons which would carry with him throughout his life.

Within three days of his arrival, Karel and his retinue were almost poisoned by an agent interfering with their breakfast. Under torture, the unknown mass confessed to have been working on the orders of Azzone Visconti, to wage war on the Luxembourg polity. The strife then of the French and German noble, knightly cultures against the political rats of Italy was observed, within the perspective of the young prince. Karel had missed being poisoned simply on account of his attending of Mass upon his arrival in Pavia, receiving Communion and preferring to remain fasting. 

In Parma, Karel would meet his younger brother Jan Jindřich for the first time, and come to relish in that camaraderie. Knowing how their father was, and the death of their mother, Karel felt above all obligated to be a personable influence in his siblings’ lives. It was there that he was informed as well of his father’s departure, leaving the Luxembourg Signoria to his command, with Louis II of Vaud arranged as his chief advisor. As 1332 came in, another league against the Luxembourg possessions formed, among the lords of Milan, Verona, Ferrara, and Mantua, and Louis II would abandon Karel as his eldest daughter married Azzone Visconti. The anti-Luxembourg league besieged Modena and plundered the countryside while Karel waited, forming an army of his own to challenge them. The besiegers abandoned the attempt on October 27, withdrawing to the Castle of San Felice, while Karel finalized his levying of troops from allies and towns nearby, bringing to arms some 200 knights and 6,000 footmen. 

Karel set out with his host to meet the army raised against him, being knighted with his retinue prior to the day of battle and fighting on St. Catherine’s Day, 1332, and though he would be wounded during the course of the battle across the afternoon he would emerge triumphant, with his first battle and first victory achieved. Despite being dehorsed and nearly slain in the battle’s course, Karel attributed his survival and victory to divine grace and the intercession of St. Catherine.

Despite the victory, the demesne of the Luxembourg Signoria had still shrunk, and Jang returned to Italy with reinforcements while Karel was sent to Merano, after having founded the town of Montecarlo near Lucca. The prince would depart from Lombardy believing in the infamous treachery of Italy to be the engine which ran it, as he had experienced, and kept with him the associated memories as he returned to Bohemia.

Thank you for your open and most kind message, especially during such a difficult time. We greatly value your friendship and remai
events.fullText:
My blessed friend,

Thank you for your open and most kind message, especially during such a difficult time. We greatly value your friendship and remain open to cooperation between our realms.

However, you speak wisely in recognising that a formal declaration of alliance may, under the present circumstances, bring unnecessary suspicion and place both our houses in a delicate position. For this reason, we believe it best not to make any hasty decisions, but instead to carefully observe how the situation develops.

Should matters settle in time, we would remain open to closer cooperation between Trier and Lorraine, in the shared interest of peace, stability, and prosperity throughout the Moselle region.

Likewise, we welcome future discussions regarding regional affairs and look forward to continued friendship and cooperation.

May God grant you strength and wisdom during these difficult times.

Your friend,
Raoul d’Alsace, Duke of Lorraine

events.fullText:
Once again, a nobleman sent from Nancy arrives at the court of Paris bearing a sealed letter.

Greetings of the highest respect and esteem to King Philip VI of France,

We received Your honourable message with great gratitude and deeply appreciate Your gracious invitation to Paris. Likewise, We hold our friend Baldwin de Luxembourg in high regard. Though a direct alliance with Trier may at present be difficult to pursue given the current circumstances, We remain open to closer cooperation in the future.

||Furthermore, We are greatly honoured by Your invitation to join the campaign against King Edward III of England, and We have no doubt in Your Majesty’s eventual victory. To fight alongside Your banners would indeed be a glorious honour. Yet We must also remain mindful of the delicate position of Our Duchy and the responsibilities entrusted to Us. For this reason, We believe it wise at present to focus upon the affairs of Lorraine, strengthening the stability and prosperity of Our lands. Nevertheless, We would gladly offer Our support in the future, once Lorraine stands more firmly established.||

Should it be of interest to Your Majesty, We would be honoured, after successful developments within Our realm, to travel to the court of Paris and bring with Us the finest wine of Nancy as a gesture of friendship and goodwill.

May God protect Your Majesty, and may success accompany all Your endeavours.

Written at Nancy, under our seal, in the Year of Our Lord 1337.

Your friend and faithful neighbour,
Raoul d’Alsace, by the Grace of God Duke of Lorraine

## King of all Serbian and Maritime Lands
The cool stone of the keep leeched Stefan Dušan’s warmth through his boots. Below, his castle slept. His que
events.fullText:
## King of all Serbian and Maritime Lands
The cool stone of the keep leeched Stefan Dušan’s warmth through his boots. Below, his castle slept. His queen rested with their infant son. His Vlastela, scattered to their own baštinas, had drunk their fill and eaten their feast. All his kingdom rested, but the Young King could not. He was restless, pacing, so much demanded, and so much more required.

He watched the sun bleed its light out across the horizon, a wound of deep orange and red across the sky, the life and warmth slowly fading. The air sharpened, and the sudden chill was not merely from the evening turned to night. A shiver ran up his spine. It carried ghosts of his past, his father’s betrayal, stripping his right to Serbia. However, the warmth of pride resisted from within. Turning betrayal into triumph, he had defeated and chased down his own disgraced father. For the betrayal, Stefan Dečanski would never see a living free day again.

But a crown is a heavy, ravenous thing. Demanding, demanding, whispering, scheming. To the north, Hungary and Bosnia were wolves, picking at his weak frontiers. To the west, the Italian kingdoms and republics squatted on his maritime lands. To the east, a new found ally, and brother, Ivan Aleksandar of Bulgaria.

As he walked, a gust of wind hammered into his back, not just flowing from the north, but pushing him, pressing him south. South, to the dying, golden corpse of the Roman Empire. South, where the Greek lords squabbled over scraps, turning fertile valleys into squandered fields. South, where his Vlastela’s hungry eyes turn, whispering of glory, of plunder, of a conquest his father was too weak to seize. He would not be his father, he couldn’t be. The empire’s weakness was already proven, Macedonia had fallen to him in less than a year.

A flicker in the darkness broke his reverie. Far down the serpentine southern road, a single, bobbing light. A messenger, riding hard. Coming from the south, as if summoned by his very thoughts, bearing a future that was no longer a matter of mind.

-# Opening Post, reply coming

# Limburgs Lingering Loss

The court of the Prince-Bishop gathers in order to decide the direction of the country, bickering and the like sprawling over the sore wounds left from old feuds, Noble ambitions colliding with Merchant greed.

Walram sits troubled, a room of those who can’t look forward at new futures but settling old grudges, he drinks from his cup and settles it with a resounding thud.

“As we stand, some old marks still reside in our approach to our lands, from our troubling days of failure in Worringen, whilst I may not reside within the walls Köln, I still stand by the city and making its mark on the Rhine. For our people deserve it.

However we cannot callously take our revenge on all of those who tarnished us on that day, lest we forget we are not in control of all of Westfalen lands, with that said bridging the gap between our lands seems an order beyond us without sparking another war of the likes of Limburg.

Our reach in the East should be united, our claims of Westfalen land fufilled, the lands of Arnsberg within our grasp and I stand by our right for recompense from Mark for the war through land or wealth.

On our West, we have no worries of Jülich while I’m here, we can work in unison for a common goal while my dynasty has ties here.

For Brabant though we must find a way to unite those around us to finally settle this feud, those of Berg may desire to have their dynastic home reclaimed once again, for Holland we may find an opportunity in offering them land from Brabant. Send letters to Guillaume and Adolph hastily for these offerings of a peaceful accord.”

Both the count of Holland and Berg recieve letters, for Holland the letter presents the chance of gaining land of Brabant and for Berg the offer of reclaiming the province of Limbourg in exchange for Brabant's humiliation

**The Response to the Offer**

Adolph scrutinizes the letter, weighing the promise from Koln’s esteemed halls.

Adolph sees in the letter a way back to reclaim Limburg for his House and restore its honor. His age has changed him, yet this is an opportunity not just for himself but for an entire generation—a chance he cannot let slip away.

Adolph, with everything to gain and lose, shall march on behalf of Cologne to bring security to the Rhine and the Legacy of the Limburgs.

Frederick the Fair was elected King of the Romans on the nineteenth of October 1314 at Frankfurt by a minority of the electors. On the same
events.fullText:
# Mühldorf
Frederick the Fair was elected King of the Romans on the nineteenth of October 1314 at Frankfurt by a minority of the electors. On the same day at Sachsenhausen a separate election produced Louis of Bavaria as a rival king. The double election arose from a deadlock following the death of Henry VII the previous summer, with the Habsburg party led by the archbishop of Cologne and the Wittelsbach party led by the archbishop of Mainz. Neither side accepted the other's vote. Frederick was crowned at Bonn by the archbishop of Cologne and Louis at Aachen by the archbishop of Mainz, each holding the regalia the other lacked. The dispute resolved into eight years of intermittent war across Swabia, Bavaria, and the Rhineland. Leopold I of Austria, Frederick's younger brother, conducted most of the active campaigning between 1314 and 1322. He raised men from the Habsburg patrimony in the Aargau and the Breisgau and contracted mercenaries out of the Swiss reservoir Morgarten had not yet closed to him. His operations were essentially harassing in character. The Wittelsbach lands could not be brought to a decisive engagement and Louis himself was difficult to corner. By the summer of 1322 both sides had concluded that the next field encounter would decide the war.

-# *Pictured above is the battle of Muhldorf, showcasing the charge of Johann of Bohemia*
## Die Schlacht
The armies converged on the lower Inn in late September. Frederick crossed the river near Mühldorf with roughly fourteen hundred knights and a body of Hungarian light cavalry sent by his brother-in-law Charles Robert of Anjou. Leopold was marching east from Swabia with the larger force, perhaps two thousand horse and a substantial infantry component. Frederick did not wait for him. On the morning of the twenty-eighth of September his cavalry engaged Louis's army on the plain east of the town, before the Bavarian and Bohemian wings had fully deployed. The battle lasted most of the day, into the waning hours of the afternoon. Frederick's Hungarians broke the Bavarian left in the first hours and the Habsburg knights pressed the center. Around midday Johann of Bohemia arrived on the field with a force that had been screening the eastern approaches. Johann's intervention struck Frederick's flank while the Habsburg line was extended in pursuit. The flank collapsed first, then the center. Frederick was unhorsed and captured by Seyfried Schweppermann, a Bavarian knight whose name Louis remembered favorably for the rest of his reign. Habsburg losses were severe in knights and devastating in prisoners. Eleven hundred men of rank were taken alongside Frederick himself.

**To the Noble and Mighty Prince, Ludwig
events.fullText:
A noble envoy from Lorraine arrives at the Imperial court bearing a sealed letter from Duke Raoul d’Alsace.

**To the Noble and Mighty Prince, Ludwig IV, by the Grace of God Holy Roman Emperor, King of the Romans, We extend Our greetings and highest respect.**

We wish Your Majesty a glorious and prosperous reign and honour Your Imperial dignity with the respect owed to so great an authority.

As a young Duke, We wish to assure You of Our loyalty and commitment to the Empire. Though the people of Lorraine share long-standing customs and ties with neighbouring realms, Our Duchy remains devoted to the prosperity, order, and strength of the Empire.
We strive to govern Lorraine in such a manner that it may continue to contribute to the welfare and prosperity of the Imperial lands.

Lorraine shall remain steadfast in its duties within the Empire.
May God preserve Your Majesty in health, wisdom, and strength for many years to come.

Written at Nancy, under our seal, in the Year of Our Lord 1337.
Raoul d’Alsace, by the Grace of God Duke of Lorraine

With the Emperor of the *Germans* publicly questioning King Robert of Naples' title, "Vicar of Christ", Robe
events.fullText:
# A Crown and A Duty to Protect Italy...

With the Emperor of the *Germans* publicly questioning King Robert of Naples' title, "Vicar of Christ", Robert has decided to respond by publicly writing a letter to the Princes, republics, and independent cities of Italy, calling out the Emperor's desire to enforce his "title" while remaining absent in Italy and her affairs.

*A Letter from the King of Naples, Robert "the Peacemaker"*

"To the honorable Princes, Republics, and Faithful Cities of Italy,

The North, for some time now, has been smothered beneath the chaos of division, violence, tyranny, and the endless feuding of princes who proclaim loyalty to an *"Emperor"* absent beyond the Alps. Our homes burn to the ground, our roads fall into disrepair, our trade is falling, and the faithful Christians of Italy are left abandoned whilst the Emperor claims "authority" yet delivers neither peace nor justice for his "subjects".

Italy cannot remain forever shackled to an Emperor who has failed to defend her, failed to govern her, and failed to preserve her for her people. The Emperor speaks often of his "rights" within Italy, yet where was he when our home descended into chaos? Where was imperial justice when tyrants and warlords bled our communes, towns, and cities dry?

Italy does not cry out for distant titles, but instead for order, peace, and prosperity.

I call upon you to recognize the need for change within the peninsula. I extends my hand not in the pursuit of needless war, but in the restoration of peace, prosperity, and **Christian order** throughout Italy. The Kingdom of Naples shall not permit the peninsula to remain merely a battleground for foreign ambitions and imperial neglect. The Kingdom of Naples shall not permit the foreign influence of Our Mother Church while the absent "Emperor" remains in Germany... Until I breathe my last breath, I shall not stand for it. 

Should the Imperial Throne of the Germans prove unwilling or unable to restore peace and lawful governance to Italy, then it is only inevitable that someone else should be called to that responsibility. The Iron Crown shall not forever rest upon the brow of absence Emperors and their failure to govern responsibly. The time has now come to question the legitimacy of the Imperial claim to the Iron Crown... 

With this letter, I, Robert, King of Naples, hereby pledge my life to the defense of Italy and call upon the you princes, republics, communes, and faithful cities of Italy to side with Italy and our people, over the Empire that has failed us. To those who still side with the Empire, where is your Emperor?

Signed, 
Robert of Anjou
King of Naples, King of Jerusalem, Count of Provence, and Vicar of Italy"

TL:DR: Robert is trying to call out the Emperor for his failures in Northern Italy, specifically with the lack of peace, prosperity, and order that has occurred from Imperial absence. He is also seeking to invite members of the Ghibelline Faction to the Guelph faction through diplomacy, rather than war.

events.fullText:
## The Strife of Two Queens

Such a prize, Bohemia was, that Henri VII sent his fourteen year old son, Jang, to marry Eliška Přemsylovna, the eighteen year old princess wrapped up in the dynastic strife of the “post-Přemslyid” Bohemia. Her mother died when she was four, her father died when she was thirteen, only three of her ten siblings survived to adulthood, and her brother Václav III, the final Přemsylid king, was assassinated on the eve of his planned invasion of Poland. These events dominated her youth, wherein she resided with her aunt Kunigunde at a Praha nunnery, where she also lived with her sister-in-law Viola Alžběta Těšínská, and her stepmother Eliška-Rejčka, of the Piasts, who had been spouse to Václav II in his reign over Poland. Her suitability as consort became a political pawn in this strife-ridden landscape that almost lowered Bohemia to the extent that Brandenburg had, at just about the same time.

Jang and Eliška married at Speyer, where even amidst all the political motivations of the marriage and event, the conditions of Eliška’s life warranted a personal touch from Henri;

> “Welcome, royal child of Bohemia! Thou hast thus far been an orphan; henceforth thou shalt be my daughter, and I shall be thy father. Here is my only son, thy future husband. Forget the trials thou hast endured at home, and rejoice with us!”

The young couple arrived in their claimed country with the armed entourage Jang’s father could spare, against the forces of Heinrich von Gorizia, the reigning King in Bohemia, and Friedrich von Wettin, the Margrave of Meissen. A brief campaign in the south opened the way for a march on Praha, which fell by way of treachery among the nobility therein, and Heinrich fled to Carinthia, leaving the kingdom in the hands of the youthful pair. This was meant to be the start to a lasting, well-groomed marriage between the two, whose kingdom would find peace and stability with their reign—that was the hope, at least. [ <@215639063502258176> ]

Jang, who had been fourteen when he was married, when he became king, never grew into the role. In fact, he longed for and pursued a “lower” course, the path of the knight-errant. After having signed many privileges and rights away to the nobility to have peace in Bohemia upon his accession, Jang eventually neglected the office of kingship and his wife as well, who yet bore him a son until six years after their marriage. He departed from Bohemia and made regular travels across the breadth of Europe, participating in tourneys and lavishing in noble feasts. The constant presence of a king among these events made him popular and well-known, even received fondly in Paris where he was raised, and where his blood ties with the Capetians granted him stay. All the while he busied himself in the company of lords and ladies across Europe, Jang was oft cursed by his subjects, who knew him as terribly aloof of his responsibilities, for he only truthfully returned to Praha to replenish his purse after grand spending. Naturally with the limitations placed upon him, and the lack of impetus for the taxation of the country to be expanded, he often resorted to means of extortion, seizing from his own household, or arranging fiscal corruptions with the Church in Bohemia.

Owing to the absenteeism of Jang, his government in Bohemia fell into the hands of whatever nobility he promoted while he was in-country. Among those which stood out the most was **Jindřich z Lipé**, who had been appointed Supreme Marshal and chamberlain in 1310 before being replaced by the German party which Henri had dispatched with his son, because of his minority. His return to these offices came in 1315, after the Bohemian estates finally grew tired of the overextension of the government of the German lords, and demanded Jang oblige by the terms he signed in 1310 to field no foreigner in his government. While some rumors abound of Jindřich embezzling funds from the royal household to pay off his own creditors, he was also propagandized against by Queen Eliška, who was at odds with him becoming the lover of her stepmother, Eliška-Rejčka. Through her admittedly fading influence over the King, Eliška secured Jang’s action against Jindřich, having him arrested at Praha Castle in 1315 and charging his imprisonment to **Vilém Zajíc z Valdeka**, who became chamberlain as reward for his service. 
-# Jindřich z Lipé and Vilém Zajíc z Valdeka.

The Ronovci family from which Jindřich hailed had much support among the aristocracy and resisted his imprisonment, instigating another round of civil war in Bohemia which was only brought to an end by the diplomatic aid rendered by the archbishops of Trier and Mainz: Baldwin and Peter von Aspelt, respectively. Six months after his imprisonment began, Jindřich was released with several of his castles being turned over as collateral, and a small party of hostages. Jang, “successful” in pacifying the country, departed once again and left it in the hands of Peter von Aspelt, who would leave soon after as well amidst resentment and resistance to his rule. Succeeding him in governing Bohemia was Queen Eliška and Vilém Zajíc, though tensions between Eliška and Eliška-Rejčka arose once more after the latter married off her daughter, Anežka, without the Queen’s consent. The Queen denied the truce as it was negotiated, and went to Loket in 1317 for refuge, sending messengers to her Jang prompting his return, which he did characteristically with a conglomerate of German and French mercenaries. When his summons for the party opposing the Queen went unanswered at Praha, Jang committed himself to resolving the situation with militant force, yet in that choice even ostracized Vilém, the royal favorite. Much of the nobility agreed to armed resistance against the King, and even negotiated with **Friedrich der Schöne**, offering the Habsburg Duke the kingship of Bohemia if Jang proved too intolerable and unnegotiable.  Between Jindřich and Jang, the former was seen as a more stalwart and focused defender of Czech interests than the absentee king. [ <@190640828954181632> ]
-# Eliška-Rejčka.

With the arrayed nobility against him and his army of mercenaries, which he was likely unable to afford to sustain any longer, Jang relented and requested the intervention of **Ludwig IV** [  <@763652399784919080> ], whose oversight witnessed the **Domažlice Agreement** in 1318, which was functionally a royal capitulation to noble demands. Eliška remained unsatisfied, preferring to restore the Přemsylid project of a strong monarchy and enact it through herself, while Jang simply sought the pleasures of life abroad. In 1319 the Queen took the children to Loket, stirring the suspicions of the King, likely planted by Jindřich to bring about the downfall of his arrival. The conspiracy alleged that Eliška intended to overthrow Jang, and have their eldest son, Václav, elected in his stead as her puppet. Jang responded by laying siege to Loket, banishing Eliška and their children to Mélník, and to keep Václav himself imprisoned in Loket for some time. Jindřich exchanged some of his estates with the King for lands in Moravia, and his power rose while Vilém’s fell. However, later in that same Summer, Praha itself took up arms against the King and his party and was forced to discuss an armistice and peace after the Rožmberka joined against him. 

Vilém’s ascendancy was broken, and Jindřich returned to royal acclaim, receiving his old offices. In 1322 another conspiracy was suspected on behalf of the Queen, causing her to flee to the land of her son-in-law, Duke Heinrich XIV of Bavaria, before she was forced to return to Bohemia after Jang suspended her royal stipends. Jindřich z Lipé, with his lover Eliška-Rejčka, settled in Brno and created their own lavish court after asserting their strength in Moravia. These events, whose shadow marred the full breadth of his reign thus far in Bohemia, only polarized his knight-errant preference further, and the government of Bohemia was left in the hands of Jindřich’s allied party. 

The first son of Jang and Eliška, Václav, wound reunite with his mother briefly after her return from exile and the end of his imprisonment, before he was sent to depart to Paris for the French Court. In his confirmation therein he would take the name Karel, in honor of his godfather, the Last Capetian, Charles IV. He would be betrothed as well to Blanche de Valois, but their time together would soon end and he would continue among strangers. The death of Charles IV made his environment poorer, for Philippe VI as a profligate spender reduced the funds for little Karel’s education. Yet still, he would immerse himself in his studies at the University of Paris, excel in many languages, and forever be imprinted on by the centralized nature of the French Monarchy.
-# Queen Eliška.

Karel departed from his father’s Italian campaigns in 1333 after meeting with a party of the great lords of Bohemia, who at Merano professed the kingdom in dire straits with protracted royal absences, and made it clear that his presence was necessary. In October of 1333 he crossed the border, making his first stop at the Zbraslav Monastery, where his mother, who passed three years prior, laid. 

Karel’s childhood had been fostered in the shadow of the dispute and wars between his mother and Eliška-Rejčka, whose feuds led to much suffering across Bohemia. Those events, including his protracted stay away from home while his mother came ill and passed, only further contributed to the dissonance between father and son.
-# done

events.fullText:
## *The Eagle’s Nest in the Alps*

What had first been attempted as the grandest of dynastic projects at the present decline of the Habsburgs following Frederick’s defeat at Mühldorf, Jang sent envoys to his former rival, Heinrich von Gorizia, whose Bohemian throne he took from him, to arrange a marriage at a moment of dynastic weakness. Heinrich was poor, laden with debts, and had his daughter as his only heiress to the lands of Tyrol, Carinthia, and Carniola. Haus Gorizia was on the brink of being subsumed by inheritance, and it was yet another inheritance which brought the conflicting interests of Haus Wittelsbach, Haus Luxembourg, and Haus Habsburg against one another.

In 1327 it was arranged: the marriage of the five year old son of Jang, Jan Jindřich, to the nine year old Margaret, daughter of Heinrich. Jang’s plot was evident, to include in Haus Luxembourg’s power base the inheritance of Tyrol, Carniola, and Carinthia. As much as he had been obsessed with the Přemsylid claims to the Polish throne which he claimed to have inherited from his wife, he was without a doubt similarly interested in the acquisitions once mad bet the infamous Ottokar II, the ***Iron and Golden King***, who had ruled over the Austrian Duchies attached to Bohemia until his defeat at Marchfield. The marriage itself only came to be in 1330 with the approval of Ludwig IV, allowing the eight and twelve year old children to marry at Innsbruck, though the attendees noticed pretty early that the two had a strong dislike for one another. 

With the marriage, Haus Luxembourg laid stake to the County of Tyrol, giving it the alpine pass that Jang would infamously use for the base of his expeditions into Italy in the early 1330s. 
[ <@315868897012219904> <@545657694489542666> <@338903160246763531> ]
However, this expansion of power of course disturbed the personal interests of Ludwig IV, who perhaps on account of utilizing the Habsburgs against a shared foe, would bring about the greatest reason for the end of Jang’s Italian campaign: the inheritance disputes over the death of Heinrich von Gorizia in 1335. Instead of what Jang had set out to accomplish, Ludwig IV reneged on even the deals he made with the late Heinrich, enfeoffing the Duchy of Carinthia and the March of Carniola on Albrecht II, Duke of Austria and Styria of Haus Habsburg. Ludwig IV further intended to push his own house onto Tyrol, to nullify the marriage of Margaret and Jan Jindřich, and in response Jang sent his eldest son Karel from his post at Lucca following the [Battle of San Felice](https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545415213449216/1507701211409944608) to Meran, to act as regent for his younger brother and enforce the rights of the couple. Therein, the local Tyrolean nobility backed Karel in effect of ensuring Margaret’s inheritance was recognized and enforced.
[ <@763652399784919080> <@190640828954181632> ]

The Kaiser’s choice to promise the lion’s share of the **Gorizian Inheritance** to the Habsburgs and then split the County of Tyrol between themselves resulted in Jang’s presence at the Congress of Viségrad, and his subsequent campaign into Austria against Albrecht II. Peace was signed between Haus Habsburg and Haus Wittelsbach on October 9 1336, when Jang renounced his claims to Carinthia and Carniola, and Albrecht recognized Margaret and Jan Jindřich in Tyrol. Jan’s haughty attitude and incompetence at rule quickly turned much of the Tyrolean aristocracy against him, and upon the frustration of Karel’s attempts at mediating among the estates as regent, he would depart from Tyrol to join his father on the Lithuanian Crusade, leaving Jan and Margaret to their own devices.

Now, the unhappy couple remain in Tyrol, with Jan being seemingly universally despised within his “own” lands. Margaret successfully used her ploy with the Luxembourgs for her own ends: her ascension to the comital throne against the disposition of those attempting to carve apart her fief. Now, the last obstacle to her ambitions remains at her side, wed, and even Karel expects no good news to emerge from the county in lieu of their relationship.
-# The Count and Countess of Tyrol in 1337.

There are many different ideas as to where the name Perm comes from; some say it refers to the great wooded hills of these lands, othe
events.fullText:
**Great Perm**

There are many different ideas as to where the name Perm comes from; some say it refers to the great wooded hills of these lands, others say it refers to the merchants who trade in fur and salt, others still say it is called Perm because it lies on the edge of the known world - perhaps it is all three, for certainly those three things define the country.

In the capital Ćerdin sits the Prince of the Komi on a fur-clad wooden throne in a wooden hall, yet he is no King - no - for he is merely the greatest among equals; the best suited of all the Komi chieftains to treat with foreign emissaries and oversee the country’s markets. For over a century now this is how Perm has been governed, and thus far it has been adequate. When crisis has occurred (overzealous Rus’, Bulghars or Tatars demanding more than their due) the chiefs have rallied under the prince and secured their rights and lands for the generations to come… 

*…but change is in the air.*

Novgorod grows greedier and greedier, while the descendants of the Great Khan cling to what power they still have in Sarai. Storms brew in the lands to the west and the south… and should the winds blow north, the Komi peoples may find themselves stuck in a blizzard unlike anything seen before. 
If Perm is to survive the coming centuries, change is needed; the chieftains must stop their bickering and the tribes must call each other friends not just during eras of crisis as in ages past, but *always* be ready to help eachother wether with bow and arrow or dried meat and drink in hand.

*Painting by Sergey Bartold, 1933*

Established after a long period of conflict in 1323 the treaty was signed to establish peace and define a frontier of Swedish territory in Finland. Novgorod recognizing the Swedish possession of some Karelian Districts. The Swedish still lay claim to these lands and migration has only been increasing as more and more families cross the Baltic to settle there.

Church and Noble backed settlement arise in the area, many hoping to establish control in the region. Viborg remains the center of political power in the region, as the easternmost Swedish outpost the castle houses a garrison of soldiers it also serves as the seat of power of the local ruler, surrounded by a settlement that has been increasing in size attracting merchants and trade. Catholic Christians traders compete with the Traders of Novgorod creating tensions in the region. While the potential for conflict increases King Magnus, doesn't wish to fan the flames instead focusing on calming the unrest of the overextended Swedish Realm.

events.fullText:
## The Lion of the Danube ##

The history of the Hungarians can be summed up as nothing less than the odyssey of a storied people. Hailing from Magyar nomads that slept in yurts, drank fermented goat's milk, and traversed the great Eurasian Steppe, the Hungarian peoples would go on to forge their own destiny and carve out a home for themselves. Fleeing westward across the Great Steppe from hostile hordes, the Árpád leaders of the Hungarians would shepherd their flock into the lands of the Pannonian basin and settle themselves along the long Danube River. Henceforth, the Lion of the Danube had been born.

However, this lion was only a cub surrounded by greater threats, with Bulgarians, Franks, Romans, and others posing a sustained threat and all willing to snuff out the cub if it became an inconvenience. In response, the cub had to grow quickly. Henceforth, the Hungarians would never allow themselves to fall into defence and would come to control the entire Carpathian basin. This, however, was an untenable situation. Although the young lion was full of energy and spirit, it could not defend itself surrounded by enemies. As such, first Géza, then his son Stephen I Vajk Árpád, would seek to integrate Hungary into the Christian world. The efforts of the latter would bear fruit, as by the 11th century, Stephen I had been proclaimed a saint and the Hungarian realm recognized as an Apostolic kingdom. As such, the lion had fully matured and the Kingdom of Hungary had cemented itself into the European world.

events.fullText:
## The Shield of Christendom ##

By the beginning of the 14th century, the Hungarian realm had greatly changed since the days of Saint Stephen I. The lion, once youthful and strong, was now more aged and scarred from fierce battles against many enemies. Most of these scars had been inflicted by the fearsome and cruel Tatars of the east, hellspawn that rode on horseback and burned, pillaged, and looted all that they saw, their eyes full of avarice and cruelty. However, the Lion of Hungary would not allow the Tatar threat to pass further into Europe. The Lion would stand in defense of the innocent and become a shield of Christendom.

However, the Árpáds no longer ruled Hungary. The long lineage of Saint Stephen had gone extinct, with Andrew III marking the end of the dynasty. Now a new dynasty ruled from Buda. King Charles I of the Capetian House of Anjou would win the game of thrones that had ensued and be crowned King of Hungary, Croatia, and Dalmatia. His ascension marked the rise of the first foreign dynasty to rule the Hungarian realm. He consolidated the realm and put the troublesome nobility in its place. By 1337, King Charles had cemented his Angevin rule over the Hungarian realm. With links to the royalty of Poland, Naples, and France, the new king made himself into a great and powerful monarch. His ascension marked a turning point for the Hungarian realm, a realm as diverse as an intricate tapestry. Despite this, threats remained. The Balkan peninsula remained ever restless, and the decaying remains of the Byzantine Empire had only exacerbated the instability of the peninsula. The Tatars to the east still remained an ever-present, though diminished, threat. However, one could not deny that the Lion of the Danube and the Shield of Christendom stood as strong as ever.

Éljen Magyarország ~ Vivat Hungaria

How will this impact the Herring market?

After the collapse of Royal Power in Denmark, much of their lands were seized by creditors. Scania, one of the richest territories of Denmark, a seat of church power, sest of bishop of Denmark, and a vital trading market, known as the largest Herring market in northern Europe was now held by the counts of Holstein. But this did not last long, the local elites were not content under the rule of these German counts and rebelled, preferring to be ruled rather by Sweden. This resulted in a deal and purchase and takeover of Scania by the Swedish Crown. While truly a magnificent and prestigious prize, the consequence of the large sum spent, put a grave strain on the finance of the kingdom resulting in raised taxes and unrest.

It is almost a rule that following a successful crusade, the crusaders would turn on each other over land and benefits. The No
events.fullText:
**Troubles in Estonia**
It is almost a rule that following a successful crusade, the crusaders would turn on each other over land and benefits. The Northern Crusades were no different. Denmark had hoped to gain far more from Wendia and the Baltic than just Estonia but their attempts to take lands further were met with crushing defeat at the hands of the Teutonic knights. 

Today, a century later, even their position in Estonia is tenuous. Disputes between the Danish Bishop of Reval and the pro-German Captain Marquard Breide seem to tilt the balance ever in the favour of the Germans. 

And yet, even with real power rapidly changing hands, de facto loss of the territory seems difficult for the Germans to achieve. King Christopher had made a solemn pledge to the Papacy that Denmark would never sell or lose their Baltic lands. As servants of the God, the Teutons seem unwilling to make more aggressive moves than the intrigues already in play. 

Nevertheless, Estonia must see reform if it is to remain in Danish hands. Without it, it will surely be lost to local or Teutonic foes.

**The Crimes of Cristopher**
Successive generations of Danish Kings have long financed ill conceived squabbles through the pawning of provinces and ca
events.fullText:
**The Crimes of Cristopher**
Successive generations of Danish Kings have long financed ill conceived squabbles through the pawning of provinces and castles. This trading of future income for gold in the present would perhaps have mattered little if the crown could pay its debts but the failure to utilize these funds for anything constructive has over the course of a century resulted in a Kingdom owned not by its King but by Germans counts. 

None are worse, however, than King Christopher II. While he did accomplish great things in the northern crusades, he failed to finance his work through appropriate measures. His inability to repay his debts would lead to his signing of the humiliating handfæstninger which stripped him of his taxation rights and judicial authority. In his passing, Denmark would be effectively kingless with the years from 1332 to present being known as the Kingless Years.

Denmark today is a nation crushed by debts and its nobility, held together by a mere regent. Denmark, as it would seem, is over...

*King Christopher II of Denmark and the Wends*

events.fullText:
**A House of Many**


For a variety of reasons, ranging from the natural exchange that is common to all mercantile countries to the practice of Berber slavery.  Due to this multiculturalism, a diversity of religions also exists.

Following the traditional Dhimmi system, those non-Muslims within the country have been granted a special status with legal protections, some of which protect against Islamic law, some of which enshrine their land rights, and some of which allow for an establishment of local noble hierarchies, and most critically, freedom of religion.  These special statuses typically come with additional tax requirements.

This freedom of religion has even been extended to the slave class, allowing them to retain their religion, as a modest concession.  This means that they will be afforded the requirements to conduct their faith, and Catholic priests within the Dhimmi communities will be allowed to administer to the practicing slaves regularly.

This has been enshrined into law by the signature of the Sultan.  The intellectuals within the religious community generally approve of the change, however those more hard-line individuals have taken offense at the lack of forced conversions.  The long term impacts of this policy will be felt for years to come.

events.fullText:
# County of Armagnac
Under Roman rule, the lands of Armagnac were under the lands of Aquitania. Under the Merovingain dynasty, the lands stayed under the duchy of Aquitania until a hereditary county split into the lands known as [Fezensac](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/County_of_Fézensac).

In 960, Armagnac, under Bernard le Louche, became a seperate county. In the 1040 campaign to reunite Gascony, Bernard the II reunited multiple counties, including Armagnac, under the county of Gascony.

Increased pressures from French nobility and the Crown for tithes and county allegiance, as tensions with England enflame, have made the Gascon region disgruntled and wary of French orders. Loyalty stirs at an all time low.

events.fullText:
# Centule IV

Followed by the untimely death of Joan d'Armagnac on Februrary 17th, 1337, a young Centule IV of Astarac has claimed the Armagnac seat. Through his mother, Mathe d'Armagnac, daughter of Geraud II, viscount of Fezensac, the power vacuum became sealed with control of the county.

Centule IV also maintains claims, through maternal heritage, to the counties Astarac and Foix. His distant relative, Bernat the 5th, sent message to his mother that, when he comes of age, Centule IV should take the seat in Astarac, but plots and schemes by the French nobles to turn the southern counties over to Northern French bloodlines has resulted in the seat being taken by the de Comminges family and its bastard heir.

Centule the 4th remains warry of the French nobility, unlike his members of the court who despise the ruling class. He has taken over in a time of strife, and at such a young age. Time will tell how agreeable the southern provinces may remain

## Armagnac Disloyalty

Count Centule IV is wary of your campaign, King Phillip. I grow tired of my brothers and fathers blood being split for French power and dominance. I grow weary of false promises of the stoppage of noble sackings of my holdings for "tithes"

If the King wishes the county of Armagnac to march for Gascon's freedom, then he must also receive adequate compensation for his people's blood, toil, and strife. Those in the North will no nothing of the pain and blood shed in the South of France

||If the campaign is successful, the County of Armagnac will be returned his rightful claim of the county of Astarac under his hereditary rights, as well the spoils and lands of anything captured.||

If these terms are not agreeable, you might find some other compromise, lest the English King offers a better deal

Your Vassal,

Centule IV

https://discord.com/channels/507915886477312023/1505545492736639056/1506063474017701929



The Orsini delegate is welcomed at Wawel Castle, the seat of power for the Polish monarchy. An unusual situation has emerged where the Bishop of Krakow Jan Grot has excommunicated King Casimir III for preferring negotiations with the Teutonic Order for Dobrzyń Land and Kuyavia rather then war. The king has subsequently accused the the Bishop of revealing state secrets, inciting rebellion, and abusing ecclesiastical benefices and has previously written to the Pope to request that pressure be put on the bishop to relent with his obstinancy. 

This is only the first matter to discuss. King Casimir also hopes to bring up the matter of the negotiations with the Teutonic Order to gain papal support for the Teutons to end their occupation. Furthermore there is the Lithuanian question, where the king hopes that through Polish diplomacy and tact Christianisation can be achieved rather then through the sword as promoted by the Germans. 

Much to discuss. But first, a feast in Wawel Castle! Pheasants, boars, bison and wine will be prepared for the noble man of Orsini!

The Hanseatic League currently exerts a great amount of influence over the Baltic sea. This influence, alongside historic monetary support to the order, creates the basis of good relations between the various Wendish cities and the Prussian and Livonian trading cities of Danzig, Konigsberg and Riga.

Teutonic commercial interests in England and the Low Countries also require significant cooperation with Hanseatic ports and fleets. **In Defense of the People**

Long have the Komi people hoped for a peaceful resolution to the situation in Vyatka land but it seems the hopes of the common folk have been for not; brigands for Novgorods have established themselves fully in Vyatka, erecting stockades in Vyatka town. Meanwhile their raids only grow more ambitous, more greedy, and after a raid deep into Perm lands along the Kama a council has been called in Ćerdin to resolve the issue - the consensus is clear:

*Vyatka must be pacified!*

The Komi chieftains are in agreement, and with the approval of the Udmurts, Besermyans and Chikyalik leaders the course of war has been decided upon. When the troops are ready prince Yövgin' will lead the greatest warriors west of the Urals down to the Vyatka to put an end to the greed of these scum of Novgorod.

**## Orsini have made a deal with the King Philip VI of France <:rose:865579087883272192> **
@here
**Under the agreement:**

1.Avignon shall no longer remain under Church control and shall instead be granted to King Philip VI.

2. The excommunication of King Philip VI shall be revoked.

3. King Edward III of England shall be excommunicated for declaring a war of aggression against the French Crown.

4. Luigi Orsini shall reside in Paris in good faith and mutual agreement.

With this agreement, the Schism will end, and peace shall be secured. There will be no bloodshed.

|| *“To the
events.fullText:
A sealed letter arrives at the court of **Muscovy**, carried by riders from **Kyiv **beneath the banner of **Prince Fedir Riurykovych.**

|| *“To the Princes and Boyars of Muscovy, *

*For too long the lands of Rus’ have lived beneath the shadow of the Tatar yoke. Our princes are made to bow before foreign khans, our people burdened with tribute, and our birthright denied by those who neither share our faith nor our blood.*

*Across the lands of Rus’, the drums of change grow louder with every passing season. The people yearn for freedom, and many princes now look toward the future with fire in their hearts rather than fear. Though differences may stand between our realms, we remain Slavic brothers, heirs to the same ancient legacy, bound together by history, faith, and the memory of our forefathers.
*
*I ask that Muscovy stand beside Kyiv in common cause, so that together we may cast down the rule of the Horde and restore dignity to the princes of Rus’.*

*United, we may achieve what none of us could alone. " * ||

**— Prince Fedir Riurykovych, Prince of Kyiv**

events.author: Tayta
events.snippet:
Word of the riches of the west continued to reach the halls of **Kyiv **through wandering merchants and foreign travelers. 

Tales were told of **silk
events.fullText:
Word of the riches of the west continued to reach the halls of **Kyiv **through wandering merchants and foreign travelers. 

Tales were told of **silks **from distant lands, finely woven garments(**fine cloth**), exotic **fruits **unseen in the markets of Rus’, and rare spices(**saffron**) worth their weight in silver. 

Such luxuries soon caught the attention of **Prince Fedir**, who understood that wealth alone was not their only value. To stand among the courts of Europe, **Kyiv **would need not only strength, but prestige.

Of all possible markets, **Constantinople **appeared the most natural choice. The shared **Orthodox **faith offered a bond stronger than mere coin, and **Fedir **believed that friendship with the **Byzantine **merchants could bring both prosperity and legitimacy to his growing ambitions.

The lands of **Kyiv **had long been rich in **grain**, **timber**, **coal**, **wax**, and **agricultural goods**, resources greatly valued across the **Black Sea** trade routes. Seeing an opportunity, the prince ordered letters to be sent southward toward the **burgher guilds** and merchant houses of the **Byzantine world**, offering favorable trade agreements in exchange for luxury goods and closer relations between their peoples.

Now the court awaited their answer, hopeful that commerce might succeed where armies could not.

events.fullText:
## The Battle of Mosbach

As the forces of King Karel and the forces of the late Kaiser Ludwig IV of Upper Bavaria moved to make battle against Ruprecht the Red, the combined armies of the sides, embroiling the Empire in a power struggle as the electors stood divided—two Anti-Kings fielded against one another at once. Karel himself was at the field of battle, and led his army to victory, and his claim to the Empire enforced by Rudolf II’s imposition as Count-Palatine of the Rhine, at the cost of being disfigured in the battle.

Through battle, Karel has become **King of the Germans**, but must deal with the remaining resistance of the Italians, of Albrecht II, and of the Orsini aligned clergy in Germany.

events.snippet:
# THE BATTLE OF MOSBACH - THE DISFIGUREMENT OF KING KAREL AND THE CROWNING OF KAREL AS KING OF THE GERMANS # 

When the forces of the Red-Habsburgs de
events.fullText:
# THE BATTLE OF MOSBACH - THE DISFIGUREMENT OF KING KAREL AND THE CROWNING OF KAREL AS KING OF THE GERMANS # 

When the forces of the Red-Habsburgs defeated Moravian Troops at the battle of Amberg, Heidelberg began to be placed under Siege. Quickly the forces were sent to stop the siege. With Mainz troops and Savoian men supporting the battle, the two armies met in Mosbach. 

Although the forces of the Red-Habsburgs were outmanouvered by the Bohemian-Bavarian men, the battle still was close. So close that the King of Bohemia was struck down. Not dead but disfigured in the midst of battle. 

Yet this was not enough to fight back the genius of King Karel. The local forces would quickly be defeated and the Bohemian-Bavarian Alliance would win the battle. 

Yet surprisingly the "Red" Wittelsbach would redeem himself. Having become a battlefield Medic, King Karel was saved from certain death. And with it the war and battle was over. Rudolf II was once again the Ruler of the Palatinate and King Karel, when news arrived that Kaiser Ludwig IV had died, was quickly crowned King of the Germans. 

**Crowned through battle the new soon to be Kaiser was Crowned: Long live Kaiser Karel IV of Luxemburg**

events.fullText:
## The Bohemian Regency

Karel IV’s death, weakened from his disfigurement from Mosbach,  was taken by the satanic witchery of the unholy enemies against him. With his passing, and no adult child left to inherit by way of election, a party of the local Bohemian aristocracy led by **Zbygněv z Landštejn** has organized to govern the country while **kingless**. 

Now various parties gather across the realm to discuss and send missives to other dynasties of who may be elected the next king, as the electoral privileges should forbid a long-term regency…

The clearest Luxembourg candidate is Jan Jindřich, **King-Consort of Poland**, but will they vote for him? Will other contenders arise?

events.fullText:
# What remains

Corpses lie the streets, the rivers and the fields. We have paid a great penance upon our land, those who prayed, cried to the almighty lord, have survived this wrath from the heavens. We must show our devotion for which we have not proved ourselves enough.

As the barren lands must regrow, we will now lift those who have fallen, the church has declared a mission of regrowth for the ill fortuned who will show true faith.

Another opportunity strikes, as the city lays in chaos, order may yet be restored in the Prince-Bishops name for which we can regrow this city of the Rhine

**Breton Marches Aflame**

After years of internal skirmishes and town sackings between the Blois and Montfort Factions the king of France would respond to the Breton civil war with drawing in their banners to march in the name of John de Montfort. 

Nantes would halfheartedly defend against an army of ten thousand under the command Phillipe of Burgundy however after only a handful of weeks the city traders and municipal governors would open the gates with agreements of avoiding exeuctions from the crown of france. Charlez Blois forced to flee into the Armorican Massif or face the full weight of royal forces.

events.fullText:
**Duke John III ‘the Good’ is dead, the Breton War of succession has begun**  
<@460814348487753730> <@1106651545296195655> 
> ***“For God's sake leave me alone and do not trouble my spirit with such things” ***
- Duke John III last words when asked who should succeed him

 the plague left by the wars in Aquitaine would spread not only to the poor but the so called Godly, though for Duke John he had become nothing like a Godly man for some time. In his palace at Rennes he would die in maddened fits screaming at his vassals, brought about it is said by his wife's miscarriage of a son, leaving only a daughter born, making his now succession even more complex.

Leaving behind his wife Jiana of Savoy and self claimed lady of Savoy <@564196329132261376> , daughter Janned, his half brother John de Montfort and Blois married to his niece Jeanne de Penthièvre and the realm in shock. 

in such chaos the Paris parliament would announce their support (with Phillips pre-planned blessing) for surprising Montfort and Johns Niece, the two leaving for Paris to accept their recognition, though Blois enraged by the kings war with the papacy would have already left. 

***Now in 1342 Anno Domini the Lord Blois and his wife have in the name of the three year old Daughter of the former duke Janned have seized the city of Nantes, Rennes and Saint Marlo without resistance, the lords and cities governments unable to respond in such chaos. Already moving to contact king Edward the III and the English Parliament for support against the wicked french king while Montfort will require the royal french army to seize back their lands.***

events.fullText:
**Purges in Paris and Brest Alike**

in the first months of the coup in Brittany factions turn and writhe, the Count de Rohan is found dead apparently by chocking, none believe it. while in Paris many of the friends of Blois who had been there confident of his success are arrested by justices of the crown and are swiftly found of treason against the crown and true Duke of Brittany, among them Olivier IV de Clisson one of the lords of the Breton marches, his wife in a theatrical display has sworn vengeance on the King of France though few take her seriously.

Meanwhile with the Ducal power being seized Charlez Blois and his wife swiftly sends messages with the ducal seal across France calling upon vassals to fight back against royal interference against customary law <@259426601865838592><@696548460094029914> .While the holy knight calls upon the pope who was spited by the french king for support of coin or anything else betwen <@150411715757080576> .

May god have mercy upon Brittany for the french king shall surely have none.

events.fullText:
# Italeinzug - Stephan's Coronation as King of Italy
On the 17th December, the Year of Our Lord 1351, Stephan would arrive at Milan, with the invitation of Signore Azzo Visconti.

Surrounded by staunch loyalists, King Stephan would be crowned **The King of Italy**. 

One more crown left... or maybe not?

<@137101075411894272> <@306458253674545153> <@112960565344804864> <@271318019052011532> <@763652399784919080> <@215639063502258176> <@190640828954181632> <@222439858927435776> <@564196329132261376> <@261290667106369536> <@385875387608989714> <@792904515795550218> <@420614608399761419> <@883471267506843668> <@545657694489542666> <@170593650584059904>

### Italienzug - Maybe Not.

Satisfied with his coronation in Milan, Stephan returns north, fearing that a coronation in Rome would only result in his demise.

events.fullText:
**Rennes Saved by a Saint and Black Prince **
<@1106651545296195655>

Bad weather in Devon combined with doubts in the English Parliament would see English reinforcements slow going, Blois forces suffering heavy losses on the fields of Blain in an attempt to crossguard the duke of Burgundy outriders penetrating into the Brittany western forests. 

Yet Four Months into the royal campaign at last Edward the Black Prince would in a risky maneuver land a fleet at the harsh rocky coast at Saint Marlo, outflanking the Burgundian and other ducal forces to relive the Château de Fougères, Charlez de Blois meeting the English forces on march and smashing French Forces outside the great fortress. Saving Blois and Brittany from French encroachment and seeing many a dead french knight and lord on the fields.

events.fullText:
### A union between House Valois and House Savoié
The sister to the young Duke Amedé the Green, weds the newly crowned King Phillipe de Valois, rightful ruler of all France. They are wed in Paris, further deepening the relations between the two Houses and more closely aligning the Savoyard domain with the French Crown.
